comment: If a user has a non-empty Avatar path in the DB but the file no longer exists in source storage (e.g., was manually deleted), storage.Copy returns an error that propagates through db.Iterate and aborts the entire migration. The guard on line 113 fixes the empty-path case, but a missing-file case still causes a hard failure. Consider checking os.IsNotExist on the returned error, logging a warning, and continuing rather than aborting. The same applies to migrateRepoAvatars on line 126.

If C_FindObjects fails, the function returns early without calling C_FindObjectsFinal. Per the PKCS#11 spec, C_FindObjectsFinal must always be called to terminate a find operation after C_FindObjectsInit succeeds, even on error. This leaves the session in an inconsistent state that may prevent future find operations. Call C_FindObjectsFinal before returning.

object_class_to_string() returns NULL for unknown CK_OBJECT_CLASS values. Passing NULL as a %s argument to log_debug_errno/log_warning is undefined behavior in C (though many implementations print "(null)"). Since class is a CK_ULONG that could hold any vendor-defined value, an unknown class is plausible. Use a fallback such as: `object_class_to_string(class) ?: "<unknown>"`.
Same NULL-for-%s issue as above: object_class_to_string() can return NULL for unexpected class values, causing undefined behavior in the log_warning format string.

The lock is released immediately after reading `c.minCommitTS`, but `c.forUpdateTS` is read on this line (and again written into the request at line 81) without holding `c.mu`. If `forUpdateTS` were ever modified concurrently by a batch handler (similar to how `minCommitTS` is updated), this would be a race. Currently `forUpdateTS` appears to be immutable during prewrite, so this is not an active bug, but the asymmetry is fragile. Consider reading all fields that appear in the request under a single lock scope, or add a comment clarifying that `forUpdateTS` and `startTS` are read-only during the parallel prewrite phase.

The `break` here causes control to fall through to `return IslandWindow::MessageHandler(message, wParam, lParam)`, which routes to `BaseWindow::MessageHandler` -> `HandleDpiChange`. That function calls `SetWindowPos`, which triggers `WM_SIZE` -> `OnResize` -> `OnSize` a second time with the same suggested dimensions. So `OnSize` is invoked twice per `WM_DPICHANGED`: once by this new code (before the window is actually repositioned), and once via the `WM_SIZE` raised by `HandleDpiChange`'s `SetWindowPos` call. The duplicate call is harmless since `OnSize` is idempotent, but the intent is subtle. Consider returning 0 after calling `OnSize` and then manually calling `HandleDpiChange` (or `SetWindowPos`) to make the flow explicit, or add a comment explaining the intentional fall-through.

`OnSize` is called here before `HandleDpiChange` runs (since it's invoked via the parent `MessageHandler` afterward). `HandleDpiChange` updates `_currentDpi` only after it calls `SetWindowPos`. Code in `_HandleWindowPosChanging` (lines 761, 771) compares against `_currentDpi` when enforcing maximum window bounds. If any operation triggered by this early `OnSize` indirectly causes `WM_WINDOWPOSCHANGING` on the main HWND, `_currentDpi` will still be the old value, leading to incorrect DPI-scaled maximum size enforcement. The new DPI is available in `wParam` (via `HIWORD(wParam)`) and could be passed down or used to update `_currentDpi` eagerly before calling `OnSize`.

The unique_id is set to just `description.key` (e.g. `"status"`), which is not globally unique. If a user configures two SABnzbd instances, all sensors from both entries will share the same unique IDs, causing entity registry conflicts. Fix: incorporate the config entry ID, e.g. `f"{config_entry_id}_{description.key}"`. This requires passing the entry ID (available in `async_setup_entry` as `config_entry.entry_id`) into the constructor.

Using `identifiers={(DOMAIN, DOMAIN)}` as a hardcoded device identifier means all SABnzbd config entries map to the same device. With multiple configured servers, sensors from different servers would be merged into a single device entry. The identifier should be unique per config entry, e.g. `(DOMAIN, config_entry.entry_id)`, which also requires passing it into the constructor.

The test is being skipped rather than fixed, leaving inline worker asset inlining completely unverified. The TODO comment acknowledges the feature is broken but defers resolution indefinitely. Skipped tests are often forgotten and can mask regressions. If the fix in this PR intentionally breaks this case as a trade-off, the commit/PR description should document that, and a follow-up issue should be tracked. Otherwise, the test should be fixed or the failing assertion adjusted to reflect the new expected behavior.

toHaveScreenshot takes a single screenshot and delegates to synchronous toMatchSnapshot. The feature requirement explicitly states it should auto-retry until the screenshot matches the golden. Without retry logic here, transient rendering differences (animations, late-loading content) will immediately fail the assertion rather than waiting for the page to settle.

When nameOrOptions is an object (e.g. { name: 'foo.jpg', threshold: 0.3 }), its snapshot-specific keys (name, threshold) are spread into screenshotOptions and forwarded to page.screenshot(). These are not valid PageScreenshotOptions and should be stripped before the screenshot call. Additionally, the type inference below (lines 281-284) has no branch for the object form, so { name: 'foo.jpg' } would not have its type inferred as 'jpeg'.

The description for omitBackground is a copy-paste of the overall method description and is completely wrong. It should say something like: 'Hides default white background and allows capturing screenshots with transparency.'

Typo in enum variant name: `EndBesetFitParenthesize` should be `EndBestFitParenthesize`. This is misspelled consistently throughout the codebase (builders.rs, document.rs, printer/mod.rs), so it won't cause a compile error, but it is a permanent naming mistake that will be confusing and require a future rename.

The return value of `f.write_element(...)` is not propagated with `?`. The subsequent `Arguments::from(&self.content).fmt(f)?` does use `?`, so if `write_element` can fail (returns `FormatResult`), errors from the start and end tags are silently dropped. Check whether `write_element` is infallible; if not, add `?` to both calls.

The `quick_test` scratch test was left with unrelated debug content—a raw transformer model `return` statement—rather than a meaningful regression case for this PR's changes. This should either be replaced with a test that exercises `best_fit_parenthesize` behaviour or removed.

The `enclosing.pop()` result is matched with `if let Some(Enclosing::BestFitParenthesize { expanded })`, so a mismatched or missing enclosing frame (e.g. malformed IR) is silently ignored and `expands` is left unchanged. A debug assertion or explicit error for the unexpected case would make IR construction bugs easier to catch.

Missing `encodeValuesOnly: true` option. The old code in ListViewPage.tsx used `{ encode: true, encodeValuesOnly: true }`, which preserves bracket notation in keys (e.g., `filters[name][$eq]=val`). Without it, qs default behavior encodes brackets too, producing `filters%5Bname%5D%5B%24eq%5D=val`. While Strapi's backend qs parser should decode these correctly, it's an unintentional behavioral change that could cause subtle breakage with complex nested filters if any middleware or server config does not fully decode percent-encoded key characters before parsing.

The string shortcut bypasses all encoding and validation. Any caller that passes a raw (non-encoded) string would produce a malformed URL. The current only caller (`stringify(..., { encode: true })` in documents.ts) is safe, but this new code path establishes a convention of passing pre-encoded strings through `config.params`, which is easy for future callers to misuse. Consider adding a comment that callers must pass a fully percent-encoded query string.

This creates a dense O(n) array for every scalar comparison, defeating sparsity for large arrays. The previous bug was that wrong indices were reused, but the indices could be recomputed without materializing the full dense array: compute `op(self.sp_values, other)`, keep only entries that differ from `fill_value`, and build a new sparse index from those positions. The `_unary_method` at line 1702–1706 shows the existing pattern for selectively avoiding dense reconstruction.

Dropping `base.BaseComparisonOpsTests` from the inheritance silently removes whatever comparison tests that base class defines. If those tests are now invalid or superseded, they should be explicitly overridden with a `pytest.mark.skip` or a comment explaining why they no longer apply.

The old `_compare_other` also verified the Series-level path (`op(pd.Series(data), other)`). This is now removed entirely, leaving no test coverage for scalar comparisons on a Series backed by a SparseArray, which is a distinct code path.

`np.all(op(scalar, other))` is semantically wrong for computing a scalar fill_value: `np.all` on a scalar is harmless, but if `other` is ever an array-like (which the scalar branch doesn’t guard against), it silently reduces to a single bool instead of raising an error. Plain `bool(op(data_for_compare.fill_value, other))` or just the bare expression is clearer and safer.

The prefix comparison uses `lastFrameIndex` from the *nested* selector to slice both arrays, but never verifies that the outer selector's frame sequence also ends at that same depth. If the outer navigates deeper than the nested (e.g. outer: frame1 → frame2 → button, nested: frame1 → div), `selectorPartsEqual` succeeds at index 0 (frame1 matches frame1) and strips frame1 from the nested. The `or=[div]` is then evaluated inside frame2 instead of frame1 — a silently wrong result. The fix should additionally confirm that the outer selector has no further `enter-frame` parts beyond `lastFrameIndex`, e.g. by verifying that the count of `enter-frame` parts in `parts` equals `lastFrameIndex + 1`.

This validation only catches `enter-frame` parts that are still present inside a nested selector after `parseSelector` runs. In the frame-depth-mismatch case (outer goes deeper than nested), `parseSelector` already strips the frame prefix from the nested body, leaving no `enter-frame` there. The validation therefore passes silently, and the incorrect frame context is never detected at runtime. The defensive check here should be a last resort, not the sole guard — the stripping logic in `parseSelector` must be corrected first.

If `nested.body.parsed.capture` is defined (e.g. from a `*=` capture inside the inner selector), splicing parts invalidates that index. Parts before `lastFrameIndex + 1` are removed but `capture` is not adjusted. `splitSelectorByFrame` relies on `capture` being a valid index into `parts`, so a stale value could produce an incorrect capture offset or trigger the 'Can not capture the selector before diving into the frame' error for a valid selector. Should decrement `capture` by `lastFrameIndex + 1` (or clear it if it falls within the removed range) after the splice.

The `StreamWriterCallback` property exposes a public setter without null validation. The constructor guards against null, but code can later assign null via the setter (e.g. `result.StreamWriterCallback = null!`), causing a NullReferenceException at execution time. Consider using an `init`-only accessor, or add an `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull` guard in the setter.

`ExecutingFileResultWithNoFileName` declares `fileDownloadName` as non-nullable `string`, but it is called with `fileResult.FileDownloadName` which is `string?`. In this nullable-enabled project this produces a CS8604 warning and can silently pass null where a non-null value is expected. Change the parameter to `string?`.

`Debug.Assert(range == null)` is stripped in release builds. If `EnableRangeProcessing` is set to `true` on a `PushFileStreamResult` (inherited from `FileResult`) and `SetHeadersAndLog` returns a non-null range, the full response body is silently sent regardless of the range in production. Either explicitly throw an `InvalidOperationException` instead of using `Debug.Assert`, or document/enforce that `EnableRangeProcessing` is unsupported for this result type (e.g. override the property to always return `false`).

Tariff value 0 (off-peak) is falsy, so `if device.current_tariff` returns None instead of '0', making the off-peak tariff always show as unknown. Additionally, the options list ['1', '2'] is wrong: the library docstring and implementation document the value as 0 or 1 (not 1 or 2). Fix: use `if device.current_tariff is not None` and change options to ["0", "1"].

ENUM sensors require `state` translations for each option in strings.json. The `active_tariff` entry only has `name` but is missing a `state` block (e.g. `"state": {"0": "Low", "1": "High"}`). Without these, HA's hassfest validator will reject the integration.

The test device fixture has firmware '1.4.2-EL', which evaluates to 1.4 < 1.5, so `supports_phases` is False and `fetch_phase_api` is never called. The `/f` mock added to `__init__.py` is dead code, and all three new sensors are only tested in the 'no data / unknown' path. A test with firmware >= 1.5 is needed to verify the sensors actually return values from the phase API.

The `finally` block unconditionally calls `unlock()`, but if `lockInterruptibly()` throws `InterruptedException` (i.e., the lock was never acquired), this will throw `IllegalMonitorStateException`. The lock must only be unlocked when it was successfully acquired. The correct pattern is the nested try approach used in `persist()` below (lines 93-108): place the unlock in an inner `finally` that is only reachable after a successful lock acquisition, and keep the `InterruptedException` catch in the outer try. Alternatively, use a `boolean locked = false` guard.

canBeSentToBin is determined by checking only the first item's path via FirstOrDefault(). For a batch containing files on both regular drives and network drives, if the first item is on a drive with a recycle bin, canBeSentToBin will be true for the whole batch — suppressing the dialog under the new PermanentOnly policy even though some files will be permanently deleted. Consider using All() over source paths instead: `source.All(item => RecycleBinHelpers.HasRecycleBin(item.Path))`.

The condition mixes `||` and `&&` without explicit parentheses around the PermanentOnly sub-clause. While C# operator precedence (&&  > ||) makes this logically correct, wrapping `showDialog is DeleteConfirmationPolicies.PermanentOnly && (permanently || !canBeSentToBin)` in parentheses would make the intent explicit and reduce the risk of a future edit misreading or incorrectly modifying the condition.

The error message 'Only https is supported' is inaccurate. `ParsedUrl::Archive` matches both `http://` and `https://` URLs, so valid http URLs would never hit this branch. More importantly, if the user passes a `git+https://` or `file://` URL, the error message misleads them into thinking the scheme is the problem rather than the URL type. A clearer message would be: 'URL must be an archive URL (http/https), not a git or local file URL'.

`subdirectory` is semantically meaningless for a built wheel distribution (`DirectUrlBuiltDist`). Wheels are self-contained archives; a subdirectory fragment on a `.whl` URL is not valid per PEP 508. The field is populated from `ParsedUrl::Archive.subdirectory` regardless of whether the URL is a wheel or a source archive, so it will always be `None` for wheels in practice—but its presence invites incorrect downstream use. Consider either asserting it is `None` when constructing a `DirectUrlBuiltDist`, or omitting the field from the struct entirely.

The `editable` flag from `RequirementSource::Path` is silently discarded and hardcoded to `false` when constructing the `Dist`. The TODO comment acknowledges uncertainty about whether this matters. If an editable path dependency reaches this lookahead resolver, it will be treated as a regular (non-editable) install during metadata resolution, which could produce a `Dist` that is inconsistent with how the package will ultimately be installed. The intent should be clarified and, if editable installs can reach this code path, `editable` should be threaded through.

`parser_version` has multiple unhandled failure points that will crash `rubocop --version` (with `-d`): (1) `ConfigFinder.find_config_path` can raise if no config is found, (2) `File.read` can raise `Errno::ENOENT`/`EACCES`, (3) `YAML.safe_load` can raise on malformed YAML, and (4) `require 'prism'` raises `LoadError` since `prism` is explicitly not a runtime dependency. The existing `extension_versions` method (line 74) shows the correct pattern of rescuing `LoadError`. Wrap the whole method in a rescue or at minimum rescue `LoadError` around the `require 'prism'` call and provide a user-friendly error message.

`parser_engine` accepts any arbitrary string without validation—an invalid value like `ParserEngine: foo_bar` silently becomes `:foo_bar` and only causes a cryptic failure later at parse time. The `validate`/`validate_after_resolution` methods don't check this. Add validation here (or in `validate`) that raises `ValidationError` for any value outside `[:parser_whitequark, :parser_prism]`.

Incorrect command: `bundle exec prism_spec` is not a valid standalone executable. `prism_spec` is a Rake task (defined in `tasks/spec_runner.rake`), so the command should be `bundle exec rake prism_spec`. The same line in `development.adoc` later correctly says `bundle exec rake` for the default task.

These two lines implement a subtle COM refcount workaround with no explanation. Without a comment, future maintainers are likely to remove them as apparently wrong code (an AddRef with no matching Release, and a Release with no matching AddRef). At minimum, add a comment referencing the C++/WinRT bug (bug 22116519) and explaining: (1) that during composable type construction the outer object's refcount is not incremented by the inner as it should be, (2) that AddRef() corrects the outer's missing increment, and (3) that Release() on m_inner corrects the inner's spurious extra count. The PR body notes this is a workaround pending a proper fix—that context belongs in the source code too.

Missing `config_zones(hass)` call. The original setUp always configured zone.home/zone.work for every test. This test uses `"zone": "home"` in its proximity config and validates `dir_of_travel == "stationary"` with tolerance=1000. Without setting up zone.home (lat=2.1, lng=1.1), the proximity component will use a different home-zone reference point for its distance math. Comparable tests like `test_device_tracker_test1_awayfurther` and `test_device_tracker_test1_awaycloser` both call `config_zones` before setup; this one should too.

Missing `config_zones(hass)` call. This test uses both `"zone": "home"` and `"ignored_zones": ["work"]` in the proximity config but never sets up the zone.home or zone.work states. The original unittest always had both zones pre-configured via setUp. The sibling test `test_device_tracker_test1_awayfurther_test2_first` (line 416), which has an almost identical config structure, correctly calls `config_zones`. Without zone states, the ignored-zone logic for 'work' relies on pure state-name matching rather than proper zone resolution, which differs from what the original test was verifying.

The removed `else image` branch was already dead code: at this point in the function we are inside `if isinstance(image, str):` (line 310), so `image` is always a string and `image.encode()` is always the path taken. This diff therefore has no behavioral effect. The actual fix for the `encodebytes` issue is that `base64.decodebytes()` already tolerates embedded newlines (unlike `b64decode`). If this PR is meant to introduce that fix, the relevant change (switching from `b64decode` to `decodebytes`) should be reflected in the diff; if that switch happened in a prior commit, the PR description overstates what this change does. Either way, reviewers should verify that the bug is actually resolved rather than relying on this diff alone.

`except Exception` swallows both decoding errors and PIL `Image.open` errors (e.g. corrupted image bytes that decoded successfully). When `Image.open` raises, the resulting `ValueError` message blames the image source string rather than the actual PIL error, making the failure hard to diagnose. Consider catching only `(binascii.Error, ValueError)` for the decode step, and letting PIL exceptions propagate separately.

When `frameLengthInt` is cached from a prior call (i.e., `frameLengthInt != -1`), the local `frameLength` variable is still `0` (its initialised value at line 397). Passing `frameLength` (=0) to `failOnFrameLengthLessThanInitialBytesToStrip` causes two problems: (1) `in.skipBytes(0)` is called instead of `in.skipBytes(frameLengthInt)`, so the buffer reader index is not advanced past the offending frame data; (2) the exception message incorrectly reports a frame length of 0. Fix: pass `(long) frameLengthInt` instead of `frameLength`.

There is no `try/finally` to guarantee `frameLengthInt` is reset to -1 if `extractFrame` (line 439) or the `readerIndex` update (line 440) throws an unexpected exception. If that happens the decoder will skip the length-field parsing on the next `decode` invocation and attempt to read using the stale `frameLengthInt`, corrupting the stream. Wrapping lines 434-440 in a `try/finally { frameLengthInt = -1; }` (moving the reset there) would make the state reset unconditional.

The three explicit static imports were replaced with a single wildcard `import static io.netty.util.internal.ObjectUtil.*;`. Wildcard static imports silently pick up any new static member added to `ObjectUtil` in the future, which can cause unexpected name conflicts and is typically disallowed by Netty's own Checkstyle configuration. Prefer restoring the three explicit imports.

`ApplyParameterInfo` declares `JsonTypeInfo typeInfo` as non-nullable, but the call site passes `OpenApiJsonSchemaContext.Default.GetTypeInfo(type)!`. For types not registered in the source-generated context, `GetTypeInfo` returns null and the `!` operator merely suppresses the compiler warning — it does not prevent null from being passed at runtime. The null silently propagates into `ApplyDefaultValue`, which no-ops due to its null guard, causing the default to be silently dropped. Declare the parameter as `JsonTypeInfo? typeInfo` to align the signature with the actual nullable contract.

`LastOrDefault()` is non-idiomatic for fetching a single attribute and implies intent to handle multiple instances. `DefaultValueAttribute` does not permit `AllowMultiple`, so only one can ever exist; use `FirstOrDefault()` to match the standard pattern for attribute lookup.

The test casts the default value for a `float` parameter to `OpenApiInteger`. A `float` with value `3f` serializes to the JSON token `3` (no decimal), which the OpenAPI library reads back as an integer, so the cast happens to succeed. However, this is fragile: `[DefaultValue(3.5f)]` would produce `3.5`, deserializing as `OpenApiDouble` and causing a `ClassCastException`. The assertion also masks a potential type-mismatch concern — the schema type is `number`, but its default is being represented as an integer type. The test should either use a fractional default (e.g. `3.5f`) to validate the real behavior, or assert against `OpenApiDouble`/`OpenApiFloat`.

The indexed-color check `if (_TextColor & 0xff000000)` is wrong. Regular RGB colors are stored as `(r<<24)|(g<<16)|(b<<8)`, so any color with a non-zero red channel (e.g. #FF0000) will have bit 24+ set and be misidentified as an indexed color. The indexed flag is specifically `0x01000000`, so the check should be `if ((_TextColor & 0x01000000) == 0x01000000)`, consistent with how `ToJson` and `ControlCore.cpp` do the same check.

The `ToJson` serialization for regular RGB colors is broken. Colors are stored as `(r<<24)|(g<<16)|(b<<8)`, so for any color with non-zero R, `raw` is 8 hex digits (e.g. `0xff000000` for red). `fmt::format("{:06x}", raw)` will produce "ff000000" (8 chars), but `CanConvert` only accepts strings of length 4 or 7 (i.e. `#NNN` or `#NNNNNN`). Settings with non-black RGB colors won't round-trip. Should format as `#{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}` extracting r, g, b from their respective shifted positions.

`_itow_s(color, tempBuf, 16)` implicitly converts `uint32_t color` to `int`. For any RGB color with a non-zero R channel (stored as `r<<24`), the value exceeds `INT_MAX` and is interpreted as a negative number, producing output like "-1000000" in hex instead of the intended color string. Cast to `unsigned int` or use `fmt::format` (already used elsewhere) to avoid the signed conversion.

The PowerShell command for `_TerminalAppGenerateEnableColorSelectionSettingsH` has two issues compared to the working `userDefaults` target: (1) it uses an en-dash (`–ExecutionPolicy`) instead of a hyphen (`-ExecutionPolicy`), which PowerShell won't recognize; (2) the script path `$(OpenConsoleDir)\tools\GenerateHeaderForJson.ps1` is not quoted with `"`, so paths with spaces will break. The build step will silently fail, leaving `enableColorSelection.h` ungenerated.

In `matchMode == 1`, the search used to color all matches starts from `{0, 0}` (top of buffer) and is case-insensitive. This means it will color every occurrence of the selected text in the entire buffer, not just in the visible viewport. While this may be intentional, a case-sensitive search would better match what the user selected. Also, there is no lock guard on `_activeBuffer()` in this path; if the terminal can receive output concurrently, the buffer could be modified during the search-and-color loop.

Defining a non-inline `const GUID` in a header gives it internal linkage, creating a separate copy in every translation unit that includes this header. Prefer `inline constexpr GUID` (C++17) or the Windows-standard pattern of `DEFINE_GUID` / `extern const GUID` (declared in header, defined once in a .cpp with `INITGUID`).

`m_cRef` is a plain `int` incremented/decremented with `++`/`--` in `AddRef`/`Release`, which is not thread-safe. COM reference counts should use `LONG` with `InterlockedIncrement`/`InterlockedDecrement` to avoid data races if the object is ever marshalled or accessed from multiple threads.

The return value of `ShiftEndToRange` (and the similar `ShiftStartToRange` calls on lines 378, 397, 398, and `Collapse` on line 421) is silently discarded. If these range-manipulation calls fail, the subsequent `_GetTextAndAttributeGapRange` / `_GetTextAndAttributePropertyRange` calls will operate on incorrect ranges, potentially causing text corruption or mis-routing of composition/result segments.

`FAIL_FAST_IF(!(lTextLength > 0))` guarantees the program crashes if `lTextLength <= 0`, making the immediately following `if (lTextLength > 0)` check always true and effectively dead code. Either remove the `FAIL_FAST_IF` (if `lTextLength <= 0` is a legitimate non-fatal case) or remove the redundant `if` guard.

The finalized result text before the interim character is read into `wstr` and then erased from the text store (`ClearTextInRange`), but `wstr` is never passed to `conv_area->DrawResult()`. This differs from `CompComplete` (line ~722) which does call `DrawResult`. As written, the finalized fragment is silently discarded rather than delivered to the host application.

The `if discard > math.MaxInt32` guard should be `for discard > math.MaxInt32`. If `discard` starts at more than `2 * math.MaxInt32`, the single `if` only removes one chunk, leaving `discard` still above `math.MaxInt32` when the `for discard > 0` loop runs. That loop then calls `rd.Discard(int(discard))`, which overflows on 32-bit platforms. The old code in this file correctly used a `for` loop; the refactored version regresses that behavior. Fix: change `if` to `for`.

The flat copy loop will panic if the test data directory ever contains a subdirectory, because `fs::copy` returns an error on directory entries and `.unwrap()` will panic. This is latent fragility: adding a nested test fixture (e.g., to exercise the new relative-path resolution with subdirs) would break all `line_endings` test cases silently. Consider filtering to files only with `if path.is_file() { fs::copy(...).unwrap(); }` or skipping the entry on error.

`parse_inner` is `pub`, and adding a required `requirements_dir` parameter is a breaking change for any external crate that calls it directly. If this function is only used internally (the only caller in this diff is within the same crate), it should be `pub(crate)` to avoid unintentional API surface and to make the breaking nature of future changes obvious.

`Path::parent()` returns `Some("")` (an empty path) when `requirements_txt` is a bare filename with no directory component (e.g., `Path::new("requirements.txt")`). Joining an empty path with a sub-file name resolves against CWD rather than `working_dir`. If `working_dir != CWD`, sub-file resolution will silently use the wrong base. Consider: `requirements_txt.as_ref().parent().filter(|p| !p.as_os_str().is_empty()).unwrap_or(working_dir)`.

The binary search assumes f-string ranges are non-overlapping (as documented), but this invariant will break if nested f-strings are ever supported (Python 3.12+). At that point, a range representing an outer f-string will contain a range for the inner one, and `binary_search_by` will silently return the wrong result or `Err`. A guard that detects overlapping ranges (or skips f-string inversion when nested) should be added before Python 3.12 nested f-string support lands.

`checker.generator()` is called twice in immediate succession (once for `constant`, once for `expr`). Each call allocates a new `Generator` and performs a binary search over `f_string_ranges`. Store the result in a local `let generator = checker.generator();` and pass it (or clone it) to both `unparse_*` calls. The same pattern appears in multiple files changed by this PR (e.g., `ast_unary_op.rs`, `ast_ifexp.rs`).

`get_short_circuit_edit` now accepts `checker: &Checker` solely to call `checker.generator()`. The generated content is used to REPLACE the entire BoolOp expression (which may or may not be inside an f-string). However, `checker.generator()` determines the quote style from `context.expr()`, which at this call site is the BoolOp being fixed—not necessarily an inner expression of an f-string. This should be correct in practice, but it is fragile: if `get_short_circuit_edit` is ever called with a `Checker` whose `ctx` doesn't reflect the expression being fixed, the wrong quote style will be used. A clearer API would accept a pre-computed `Generator` rather than the whole `Checker`.

`f_string_ranges` only captures `StringKind::FString | StringKind::RawFString`. If `rustpython_parser` ever introduces additional f-string `StringKind` variants (e.g. for byte f-strings or other future combinations), they will be silently excluded, causing `generator()` to fall back to the default quote style inside those f-strings. A comment here noting the exhaustiveness assumption would help future maintainers.

The error-check condition is logically flawed: it short-circuits to 'pass' when `err == nil` but `tc.err != nil`, because `err != nil` evaluates to false. If `b.Configure` unexpectedly succeeds for a 0.12 config, the test will silently pass instead of failing. The condition should separately assert that a required error was returned, e.g.: `if tc.err != nil && (err == nil || !strings.Contains(err.Error(), tc.err.Error())) { t.Fatalf(...) }`

The `async_init` call is unconditional: every HA restart creates a new SOURCE_USER flow regardless of whether all profiles already have config entries. Since `async_step_user` shows a profile-selection form rather than aborting when entries exist, this leaves a perpetually pending 'Configure' step visible in the UI after initial setup. The notification was suppressed but the root cause was not fixed. The proper fix is to guard the call: `if not hass.config_entries.async_entries(DOMAIN):` before `hass.async_create_task(...)`, or to implement early-abort logic in `async_step_user` when all configured profiles already have entries.

Once the overtime threshold is exceeded, `_handle_task_overtime_if_needed` will call `kill` and emit the warning on *every subsequent heartbeat iteration* until the process exits. There is no guard to prevent repeated kills. Add a flag (e.g. `_overtime_kill_sent: bool`) set to True after the first kill, and short-circuit on subsequent calls.

`_task_end_datetime` has no timezone enforcement in the `TaskState` Pydantic model (`end_date: datetime | None`). If a caller ever supplies a timezone-naive datetime, subtracting it from `datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)` raises `TypeError`. Either enforce UTC in the model (e.g. `AwareDatetime` or a validator) or normalize here with `.astimezone(timezone.utc)`.

`_overtime` is declared as an `attrs` instance field but is only ever read and written inside `_handle_task_overtime_if_needed`. It carries no cross-call meaning (it is recalculated from scratch each time) and unnecessarily exposes internal state. Make it a local variable inside the method.

`AirflowSkipException` sets `msg = TaskState(state=TerminalTIState.SKIPPED)` without an `end_date`, while the success path always supplies one. The supervisor currently ignores `end_date` for non-SUCCESS states, but this asymmetry will silently suppress timing data if the feature is later extended to other terminal states. Consider setting `end_date=datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)` here for consistency.

Bug: `accessToken` is read from `loginContent` (the original login response) instead of `refreshContent` (the refresh response). This means the test doesn't actually verify that the access token returned by the refresh endpoint is usable — it's testing the original login token, which would be valid regardless of whether `/refresh` works correctly. Change to `refreshContent.GetProperty("access_token").GetString()`.

Bug: `accessToken` is assigned from the `"refresh_token"` property instead of `"access_token"`. The variable holds a refresh token, not a bearer access token. The subsequent assertion that it fails as a bearer token ("The bearer token stopped working 41 hours ago") passes for the wrong reason — a refresh token is rejected as a bearer token regardless of expiry. Change to `loginContent.GetProperty("access_token").GetString()`.

Security: `configureOptions(bearerOptions)` runs after `bearerOptions.Events.OnSigningIn = HandleSigningIn<TUser>`, so a caller that sets `options.Events.OnSigningIn` in their `configureOptions` action will silently replace the security-stamp-validation handler with no warning. Users of `AddIdentityBearerToken` expect Identity security stamp checking to be active. Consider applying `configureOptions` first and then composing the Identity handler on top, or documenting clearly that overriding `OnSigningIn` in `configureOptions` disables security stamp validation.

`HandleSignInAsync` throws `NotImplementedException`, which will produce an unhandled 500 response if any code calls `HttpContext.SignInAsync(IdentityConstants.BearerAndApplicationScheme, ...)`. The `BearerAndApplicationScheme` is the default authentication scheme, so it can be reached unexpectedly. Prefer `throw new NotSupportedException("Sign in via the composite scheme is not supported; sign in via BearerScheme or ApplicationScheme directly.")` so the failure is explicit and descriptive rather than an internal implementation leak.

`subprocess.check_output` will raise `FileNotFoundError` if `/sbin/ldconfig` is absent (some minimal containers) or `CalledProcessError` if it exits non-zero, both surfacing as opaque tracebacks with no user guidance. Wrap in a try/except and fall through to the `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` check (or raise a helpful message) so users get actionable output similar to the existing NCCL error message.

`LD_LIBRARY_PATH.split(':')` can produce empty-string elements when the variable has a leading/trailing colon (a POSIX convention meaning "current directory"). `os.path.join('', lib_name)` evaluates to the bare filename, so `os.path.exists` may succeed and `locs[0]` becomes a relative path — re-introducing the exact bug this PR fixes (ldd failing on a non-absolute path). Add `if dir` to the generator condition to skip empty elements.

The substring check `lib_name in line` matches anywhere in the line, including versioned entries. For example, searching `libnccl.so.2` also matches `libnccl.so.2.19.3`, so `locs[0]` may be the versioned `.so` path rather than the symlink depending on ldconfig output order. Functionally equivalent here, but more precise matching (e.g., checking that the first token of the line equals `lib_name`) would make the function more robust for general use.

The fix correctly prevents positional input (mouse) from being captured, but `OverlayContainer` in osu-framework also exposes `BlockNonPositionalInput`, which when `true` (its typical overlay default) will silently swallow keyboard events from reaching gameplay even if `HandleNonPositionalInput` is `false`. `DefaultSongProgress` never needed to address this because it only engaged input when `AllowSeeking` was `true` (i.e. during replay/editor, not live gameplay). `LegacySongProgress` is the first subclass that is always visible during active gameplay, so if `OverlayContainer.BlockNonPositionalInput` defaults to `true`, players using keyboard (Z/X tapping) could have their key presses silently dropped when the cursor is over the progress ring. Consider also adding `protected override bool BlockNonPositionalInput => false;` here alongside the other overrides.

Critical bug: `(char**) data` passes the NULL value of `data` cast to a pointer, rather than the address of `data`. `read_full_file_full` will dereference this null pointer when trying to store the allocated buffer, causing a crash. Should be `(char**) &data`.
Typo in error message: "Failed read" should be "Failed to read".

When an included file has been deleted, `md5FromFilePath` will attempt to open a read stream for a non-existent path and throw. This path is reachable because `getInvalidations` reads the still-present `invalidated_by_update` edges (populated by the prior run) before `clearInvalidations` is called inside `runRequest`, so a deleted file's path is passed here on the re-build triggered by its deletion. The hash should handle a missing file gracefully (e.g., use a sentinel string) rather than letting the stream error propagate.

Assets restored from cache are created without an `invalidations` map (defaults to empty). When the initial cache entry hits on warm builds, `finalAssets` inherit these empty maps. For pipelines with `postProcess`, the post-process cache key is then always `getCacheKey(..., getInvalidationHash([]))` — which differs from the key written during the cold build (where `finalAssets` carry the real invalidations via the shared map from `createChildAsset`). This forces post-processing to re-execute on every second build, writing the result to the empty-hash key before the cache stabilises. Consider populating invalidations from `this.invalidations` when constructing assets in `readFromCache`, or computing the post-process read key differently.

Only the file's content hash is fed into the running MD5, not the file path itself. Two distinct included-file sets that happen to share the same content (e.g., two empty partial SCSS files at different paths) and are sorted into the same order by their paths will produce an identical invalidation hash, creating a spurious cache hit. Including the file path string before the content hash (e.g., `hash.update(invalidation.filePath).update(contentHash)`) would prevent this.

`getInvalidations` only queries `invalidated_by_update` edges. However, `addFileInvalidation` also creates an `invalidated_by_delete` edge for each included file (via `api.invalidateOnFileDelete`), and `clearInvalidations` clears both edge types. The comment acknowledges this gap ('Could add creates/deletes later'), but as a consequence, if a deletion invalidation fires for a node that has no update edge (e.g., a file that was only registered via `invalidateOnFileDelete`), the deletion will correctly re-trigger the request yet the returned invalidations list will silently omit that file. The `getInvalidationHash` call that follows may then compute an incomplete cache key.

The `config` parameter passed to `__init__` is silently discarded — `self.config` is always set to `None`. `SamsungTVWSBridge` works around this by explicitly assigning `self.config = config` in its own `__init__`, but `SamsungTVLegacyBridge` does not. As a result, `SamsungTVLegacyBridge._get_remote()` will raise `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'copy'` when `Remote(self.config.copy())` is called. Fix: change `self.config = None` to `self.config = config`.

The `acces_denied` method (also misspelled — missing an 's') is defined but never registered with the bridge via `self._bridge.register_reauth_callback(self.acces_denied)`. Without that call, `_notify_callback()` in the bridge does nothing, so the reauth config-flow is never triggered when the TV denies access. Add `self._bridge.register_reauth_callback(self.acces_denied)` after the bridge is created on line 113.

`close_remote()` unconditionally calls `self._get_remote()`, which for `SamsungTVWSBridge` creates and opens a new WebSocket connection when `_remote` is `None`. Calling this after `turn_off()` (when the TV is powering down) will attempt to reconnect and likely throw `WebSocketException` — which is not caught by the `except OSError` in `media_player.py:200`. Guard with a `None` check: `if self._remote is not None: self._remote.close(); self._remote = None`.

The pruning condition `len(cols) == 0` will also drop non-deterministic expressions such as `GROUP BY rand()`, changing query semantics silently. `LogicalSort.PruneColumns` (a few lines below) explicitly guards against this with `expression.IsMutableEffectsExpr`. The same guard should be added here: `if len(cols) == 0 && !expression.IsMutableEffectsExpr(la.GroupByItems[i]) { prune }`.

Tests only cover a non-empty table. After the fix, when all GROUP BY items are pruned the aggregation is treated as a global aggregation (no GROUP BY). For an empty table, MySQL returns 0 rows for `SELECT max(a) FROM t GROUP BY -1`, but a global aggregation returns 1 row with NULL. This semantic difference is unverified. An empty-table case should be added, e.g.: `tk.MustExec("truncate table t1"); tk.MustQuery("select count(a) from t1 group by -2").Check(testkit.Rows())` to confirm the expected row count.

The test only exercises `'serve'` mode. Since `resolveConfig` accepts both `'serve'` and `'build'` commands and they can produce different defaults, it would be worth adding a parallel assertion for `'build'` mode to confirm that `resolve.conditions` also equals `environments.client.resolve.conditions` there — otherwise a regression in build mode could go undetected.

The `{once: true}` abort listener registered on the signal is never removed when the task is cancelled via `removeEventListener`. If the signal is subsequently aborted, `task.zone.cancelTask(task)` is called on an already-`notScheduled` task, which throws in Zone.js ('task is in notScheduled state'). The new test 'should not break remove event listeners with AbortController' removes the listener but never calls `ac.abort()` afterward, so this path is untested. A guard like `if (task.state !== 'notScheduled')` inside the abort callback would prevent the throw.

When the caller passes an options object, `options` is a direct reference to that object, and `options.signal = fetchSignal` permanently mutates it, overwriting the caller's original `signal` reference. A subsequent read of `opts.signal` after the `fetch()` call will return the internal `fetchSignal` rather than the caller's original signal. Options should be shallow-cloned before mutation: `const options = args.length > 1 ? {...args[1]} : {};`.

If `fetch(url, null)` is called (browsers accept `null` as a no-op second argument), `options` becomes `null`. The subsequent `options.signal = fetchSignal` (line 36) then throws `TypeError: Cannot set properties of null`. The previous code handled this gracefully by only assigning when `signal` was truthy. The cloning fix (`{...args[1]}`) would also address this, but only if guarded: `args[1] != null ? {...args[1]} : {}`.

The abort reason is not forwarded: `ac.abort()` should be `ac.abort(signal.reason)`. Without this, code inspecting `fetchResponse.catch` or the `AbortSignal.reason` on the internal signal will see `undefined` instead of the reason set by the caller's `AbortController`. `AbortSignal.prototype.reason` is available in all modern environments that support `AbortController`.

Syntax error in test fixture: `factory(exports, require('tslib')))` has an extra `)` before the `:`, making the UMD wrapper string syntactically invalid JavaScript. TypeScript's error-recovery parser may still produce a usable AST, but the behaviour is undefined and these tests are unreliable as written. The same off-by-one exists on lines 1585 and 1596 (`factory(exports))`). Should be `factory(exports, require('tslib'))` / `factory(exports)`.

Same extra `)` syntax error as line 1576: `factory(exports))` should be `factory(exports)`. The ternary's true-branch expression is invalid, so the JS fixture fed to the TypeScript parser is malformed.

Copy-paste JSDoc error: `isSynthesizedSuperThisAssignment` has an identical description to `isSynthesizedSuperReturnStatement` ("Identifies synthesized super calls which pass function arguments directly"). The original standalone function had a distinct doc-comment describing the `var _this = _super ... || this` form. The description should be updated to describe the variable-assignment pattern to avoid confusion about what each method checks.

The fallthrough `return this.isSuperApplyCall(left)` (when the left side is NOT a `_super !== null &&` guard) broadens detection: any constructor whose *first* statement is `_super.apply(this, [args]) || this` — without the null check — is now treated as synthesized. This is intentional for the ES2015-to-ES5 downleveling case, but it also means non-TypeScript-generated code that uses `_super.apply` as the first statement would silently have its DI parameters dropped. The intent is clear from the PR context, but a brief inline comment explaining why the null-guard is absent in the downleveled case would help future maintainers distinguish the two branches.

The class docstring still lists the deprecated field parameters (year, month, quarter, day, hour, minute, second) without any deprecation notice. Since the PR is explicitly deprecating these kwargs in favor of `from_fields`, each of these parameters should be marked as deprecated (e.g., 'Deprecated since version X.X: Use PeriodIndex.from_fields instead.') or removed from the Parameters section entirely.

`from_fields` does not accept a `name` parameter, but `from_ordinals` does. This means `PeriodIndex.from_fields(..., name='idx')` raises a TypeError, creating an inconsistency between the two new constructors. A `name=None` parameter should be added and forwarded to `cls._simple_new(arr, name=name)`.

The Methods section does not list `from_fields` or `from_ordinals`, even though the class example now uses `from_fields` and these are the primary new construction APIs. They should be added to the Methods section so they appear in generated documentation.

Redundant assertion: `D_ASSERT(conflict_manager)` is always true here because the preceding `if (!conflict_manager) { ... return; }` already handles the null case. The assert is dead code and may mislead readers into thinking there is a code path where `conflict_manager` could be null at this point. It can simply be removed.

When `--editor` is used in non-tty mode without `--title`/`--body`, this generic check fires first (line 211) and returns 'must provide --title and --body...', which is misleading. The more informative editor-specific error at line 215 is never reached. Fix: add `&& !opts.EditorMode` to this condition so the editor-specific non-tty message takes precedence. The test for 'editor with non-tty' only asserts `wantsErr: true` and doesn't validate the message, so this bug is currently untested.

In editor mode, template selection is only applied when `--template` is explicitly provided. In the non-editor interactive path, `tpl.Choose()` is called to offer the user available PR templates when none is specified. If a repo has PR templates, they are silently skipped in editor mode, which is inconsistent. Consider calling `tpl.Choose()` in the `opts.Template == ""` branch here as well, using the selected template to pre-populate the editor.

The deduplication logic is inverted and will always return an empty array. Every param name is added to `seen` upfront, so `!seen.has(param.name)` is always `false` for every element, stripping all params. The set membership check must happen inside the filter callback, adding to `seen` lazily: `params.filter(param => { if (seen.has(param.name)) return false; seen.add(param.name); return true; })`.

Accessing `param.name` is unsafe for non-Identifier patterns (destructured params, rest elements, etc.). `param` is typed as `estree.Pattern`, which does not always have a `name` property. This will silently produce `undefined` keys in `seen` for complex patterns, causing those params to be incorrectly treated as duplicates of each other.

The `StartsWith` call uses the default culture-sensitive, case-sensitive comparison, which is inconsistent with the `ConcurrentDictionary`'s `OrdinalIgnoreCase` comparer. If a cache key were stored with a different casing for the ticker portion (e.g., 'btc.Bitcoin' vs 'BTC.Bitcoin'), the prefix search would fail to find it. Should use `kvp.Key.StartsWith(search, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase)` to match the dictionary's own comparison semantics.

`GetSymbol` only checks `result.Item3 != null` before returning `result.Item2`, but doesn't check `result.Item1` (the success flag). If the private `TryGetSymbol` ever returns `(false, null, null)` — for example if a null value was stored in the cache — `GetSymbol` would silently return `null` instead of throwing. The old code correctly threw when no symbol was found. Safer: check `!result.Item1` and throw `result.Item3 ?? new InvalidOperationException(...)` when it is false.

`return result.Item1` at the end of the public `TryGetSymbol` method is always `false` at that point (it is only reached when `result.Item1` is false). This is functionally correct but subtly misleading — future maintainers might assume `Item1` could be true here. Replace with `return false;` for clarity.

The prefix fallback search is not restricted to `SecurityType.Base` symbols. It will match any cached key starting with `"<ticker>."`, regardless of security type. If a non-custom symbol (e.g., a crypto pair cached under a dotted key like `"BTC.USD"`) were present, searching for `"BTC"` could return unexpected results or trigger the ambiguous-match error. Consider filtering the matches to only include Base-type symbols.

The `async_validate_config` path has the same latent bug: if an integration's `async_validate_config` also removes the domain from config (e.g. via `cv.removed`), the `[domain]` access on line 164 throws `KeyError`. That exception is NOT caught by `vol.Invalid` or `HomeAssistantError`, so it falls to the broad `except Exception` handler and surfaces as 'Unexpected error calling config validator' rather than silently succeeding. For consistency with the fix applied to the `CONFIG_SCHEMA` path, the result should be guarded: `validated = await config_validator.async_validate_config(hass, config); if domain in validated: result[domain] = validated[domain]`.

When `domain not in config` after `config_schema(config)` (i.e. the integration silently removed itself), the code falls through to the platform-schema validation section rather than `continue`-ing. In practice this is harmless because `config_per_platform` will return an empty iterator for a missing domain, but a `continue` here would make the intent explicit and avoid any future confusion or unintended side-effects if that fall-through code ever changes.

The public `UpdateJumplist` was not marked `[[nodiscard]]` while both private helpers were. Since it returns an HRESULT that callers should check, it should also carry `[[nodiscard]]` for consistency and to guard against silently ignored failures.

`ICustomDestinationList` documentation requires calling `AbortList` when the transaction cannot be committed. If `_updateProfiles` or `AddUserTasks` fails, `RETURN_IF_FAILED` returns early from the `try` block without calling either `CommitList` or `AbortList`. The COM object will be released at scope exit which may implicitly cancel the list, but explicitly calling `jumplistInstance->AbortList()` on the failure path would match the documented usage and make the intent clear.
Operator name is wrong: `@+` should be `+@`. In Ruby the unary string-unfreeze operator is written `+@` (prefix), not `@+`.
Test description says `@+` but should be `+@`. Same typo as in the changelog — the Ruby unary unfreeze operator is `+@`.
The `# good` example has two spaces after `#` instead of one, unlike every other example line in the docblock. This will render as mis-aligned in generated docs.

Since the guard on line 35 already ensures `receiver.dstr_type?`, the `uninterpolated_string?` branch can never match on `str_type?`. The check is logically harmless but slightly confusing; calling `receiver.each_descendant(:begin).none?` directly (or a dedicated `interpolated?` predicate) would be clearer and match the intent.

Passing `self.dag.timezone` unconditionally as the third argument to `datetime.combine` overrides the tzinfo of an already timezone-aware `target_time`. Python docs state that when this argument is provided it replaces the time's own tzinfo. Users supplying an aware time with a timezone different from the DAG's will silently get the wrong target datetime. The DAG timezone should only be applied when `target_time` is naive, e.g. `tzinfo=self.dag.timezone if self.target_time.utcoffset() is None else None`. The existing `test_target_time_aware` test doesn't catch this regression because it uses `pendulum.local_timezone()`, which in most CI environments equals the DAG's default UTC timezone.

The new test lacks a `@time_machine.travel` decorator, unlike the sibling `test_task_is_deferred`. The implementation calls `datetime.datetime.today()` internally, so the combined datetime depends on the real current date at test-run time. Although the assertions only check `.time()` and `.tzinfo` (and Singapore has no DST), pinning the date would make the test fully deterministic and consistent with the rest of the class.

Importing `UTC` from `pendulum.tz.timezone` reaches into a private/internal module path that may change across pendulum versions. Prefer the public constant `pendulum.UTC` (available in pendulum v2+) to avoid a fragile import.

Wrong field used: `e->ts.monotonic` is passed as the `usec` argument to `test_sd_journal_seek_realtime_usec`, but it should be `e->ts.realtime`. This is a copy-paste error from the monotonic seek block above. As written, the realtime seek tests are being called with monotonic timestamps, so they don't actually exercise the intended scenario (realtime-backward entries) and may produce incorrect expected results via `find_entry_realtime`.

All `sd_journal_seek_monotonic_usec` tests are disabled with `#if 0 // FIXME`. The PR title is "fix sd_journal_seek_monotonic_usec()" and the test infrastructure (`test_sd_journal_seek_monotonic_usec`, `find_entry_monotonic`, etc.) is present, but the calls are never compiled in. The fix therefore has no test coverage for the function it claims to fix.

`_unused_` on `test_sd_journal_seek_monotonic_usec` is a consequence of the `#if 0` block above suppressing all call sites. If the `#if 0` is eventually removed, the `_unused_` annotation should be removed too; leaving it in place will silently suppress warnings if the function is accidentally left unused again in the future.

Throwing on any unrecognised property makes all event dispatch fragile at this protocol boundary. A client with a cached (older or newer) blazor.js, or any browser extension that annotates events, will send a property the server doesn't know about and immediately crash the Blazor circuit. The old behaviour of silently skipping unknown properties is the standard resilient approach for deserialization; consider at most logging in debug mode. This throw also propagates through `ReadProperty` into DragEventArgsReader, WheelEventArgsReader, and PointerEventArgsReader.

The early `return changeArgs` inside the `value` branch combined with the new `else { throw }` creates order-dependent behaviour: an unknown property that appears *after* `value` in the JSON is silently swallowed (the early return exits the loop first), while the same unknown property appearing *before* `value` throws. JSON object property order is not guaranteed, so logically equivalent payloads will behave differently. The other readers do not have this inconsistency. Either drop the early return and let the loop run to completion (consistent with other readers), or be aware that unknown-before-value is fatal while unknown-after-value is not.

A new `JsonSerializerOptions` instance is allocated on every call to `Serialize<T>`. `JsonSerializerOptions` caches reflection/source-gen metadata internally, so discarding it each call defeats that cache and adds GC pressure. This was pre-existing, but given this PR is specifically an optimisation pass (avoiding string allocation via `SerializeToUtf8Bytes`), caching the options as a `static readonly` field would be a natural companion fix.

Spurious Windows-only import that is unused in this file. `System.Diagnostics.Eventing.Reader` is part of the Windows Event Log API and is not available on Linux/macOS, which would cause compilation failures on non-Windows platforms where LEAN is commonly run. Remove this using directive.

The AUM formatting expression `$"${Math.Round(aumCapacity/100).ToStringInvariant()}00"` is a fragile string-manipulation hack that produces incorrect output for small values. When `aumCapacity` is between 0 and 49 (inclusive), `Math.Round(aumCapacity/100)` rounds to 0, resulting in the string `"$000"` instead of a meaningful value. Prefer a proper formatting approach, e.g. `$"${Math.Round(aumCapacity, -2):N0}"` or similar, and handle the zero/near-zero case explicitly.

The original code wrapped the entire order-event handler body in a try/catch that logged errors and swallowed exceptions. That guard was removed in this refactor. Now any unhandled exception in `HandleNewOrderEvent` (e.g. `Securities[symbol]` throwing `KeyNotFoundException`, or a null `GetData<TradeBar>()` path) propagates up through the order-event dispatch mechanism. In live trading this can silently abort the handler or crash the algorithm. The Update method's own doc-comment even notes it must handle concurrent order events in live mode. A top-level try/catch (logging and returning on error) should be restored in `HandleNewOrderEvent`.

`XfaLayer.update` (called on re-renders when `this.div` is already set) is not modified in this diff and returns `undefined`. So `result` will be `undefined` on the second+ render and `result.textDivs` will throw a TypeError inside the try/catch, masking the error as a render failure. Either guard with `result?.textDivs` or ensure `XfaLayer.update` also returns `{ textDivs }`.

`_buildXfaTextContentItems` is `async` but is called without `await`. Any rejection (e.g. from `getTextContent()`) becomes an unhandled promise rejection. Additionally, if `_renderXfaLayer` is called a second time before the first invocation resolves, both calls race to call `setTextMapping`/`enable()` on the same highlighter, potentially leaving it in a confused state. Await the call or add a cancellation guard.

`setTextMapping` is called immediately after `this.textDivs = []` with `this.textContentItemsStr` as the second argument. If `render()` is ever invoked before `setTextContent`/`setTextContentStream` populates `textContentItemsStr`, the highlighter will store `null` for the strings. When `enable()` is subsequently called it throws `"Text divs and strings have not been set."` Because the reference is captured at call time, a later `setTextContent` call won't fix it. `setTextMapping` should be called (or re-called) once text content is available.

The guard condition has a commented-out clause `/* &&  xfa enabled */`. As written, any document where `_transport._htmlForXfa` is truthy will bypass the normal `streamTextContent` path and return XFA-derived text content, even if XFA is not actually enabled for search. The TODO acknowledges this needs revisiting for foreground XFA, but the incomplete condition could silently break text search for non-foreground XFA documents in the meantime. Track this in the issue rather than leaving a dead comment in a conditional.

The `unreachable!()` here will panic if a user specifies an unnamed `.whl` path requirement (e.g., `./foo-1.0-py3-none-any.whl` without a package name). `ParsedUrl::Path` with `ext: DistExtension::Wheel` is a valid state that `VerbatimParsedUrl::from_path` can produce. This code path should return a graceful error (e.g., 'Wheel files must be specified with a package name') rather than panic. Same issue exists at line 277 for `ParsedUrl::Archive`.

The new extension parser only matches `"zst"` but the old code (via `SourceDistExtension::from_str`) matched `"tar.zstd"` as `TarZstd`. Archives with a `.tar.zstd` suffix (uncommon but valid and previously supported) will now fail with an extension error. Combined with the new `warn!` added in `registry_client.rs`, this will produce log noise for any registry hosting `.tar.zstd` files. If intentional (standardising on `.tar.zst`), consider adding a more specific error message or a migration note.

`extension.to_string()` allocates a heap string on every filename parse just to get the display length. This happens twice in each parse path (lines 40/47 and 96/103). Add a `fn len(&self) -> usize` method to `SourceDistExtension` returning a constant per variant to avoid these allocations.

Previously, filenames that did not match any known distribution type were silently skipped. Now a `warn!` is emitted for every unrecognized filename. For registries that serve metadata files, checksums, or other non-distribution artifacts alongside packages, this will produce spurious warnings on every metadata fetch. Consider gating the warning on whether the filename at least looks like it could be a distribution (e.g. has a recognised stem structure) before warning.

Switching from `ThumbnailOptions.UseCurrentScale` to `ThumbnailOptions.ResizeThumbnail` removes HiDPI awareness. On high-DPI displays (e.g. 150% or 200% scale), the system won't scale up the requested size, so the returned thumbnail may be upscaled/blurry at render time. Since these are bitflags, consider combining both: `ThumbnailOptions.ResizeThumbnail | ThumbnailOptions.UseCurrentScale`.

Same HiDPI issue as the file thumbnail path above: dropping `UseCurrentScale` means folder thumbnails won't account for display scaling. Use `ThumbnailOptions.ResizeThumbnail | ThumbnailOptions.UseCurrentScale` to keep both behaviours.

`isHook` only checks `node.type === 'Identifier'` and will return `false` for `JSXIdentifier` nodes. Since `getFunctionName` can now return a `JSXIdentifier` (from the new JSXAttribute branch), hook-named JSX attributes like `<Foo useData={() => useQuery()} />` won't be classified as hook definitions — `isHook(functionName)` silently returns false, leaving the original bug unfixed for that case. `isComponentName` was updated to accept `JSXIdentifier`, but `isHook` was not. The fix should either update `isHook` to also accept `JSXIdentifier`, or have `getFunctionName` synthesize a plain `Identifier`-typed node so the existing `isHook` check works.

The parameterless Configure(BearerTokenOptions) implementation throws NotImplementedException, violating the IConfigureOptions<T> contract. While the options factory skips this path (it uses the IConfigureNamedOptions overload), any direct call via the base interface will crash. Should be a no-op body or delegate to Configure(Options.DefaultName, options).

CanUseRefreshToken reads the access token from loginContent instead of refreshContent. refreshContent is fetched on the previous line but never used, so the test actually verifies the old login token still works rather than the newly issued one from /refresh. Should be: refreshContent.GetProperty("access_token").GetString()

CanCustomizeRefreshTokenExpiration reads "refresh_token" from loginContent instead of "access_token". This assigns the refresh token value to accessToken, causing the subsequent bearer auth check to fail for the wrong reason (incompatible token format rather than expiry). Should be: loginContent.GetProperty("access_token").GetString()

The ?? throw new ArgumentException(...) guard is dead code. BearerTokenOptions.RefreshTokenProtector.get already throws InvalidOperationException when unset, so the null-coalescing operator never evaluates. Remove the check or change the getter to return null? to allow this guard to serve its intended purpose.

The return type includes UnauthorizedHttpResult but the endpoint only ever returns ChallengeHttpResult (or SignInHttpResult). UnauthorizedHttpResult is a leftover from the old implementation and causes the OpenAPI spec to declare a spurious second 401 response type. Remove UnauthorizedHttpResult from the Results<...> type parameters.

The hardcoded `dtype="datetime64[ns]"` makes this test brittle. In pandas 2.0+, the default datetime resolution changed away from nanoseconds, so this assertion could fail or silently drift. A more robust expected value would be `df.index` (the original index, which always tracks the actual default resolution), or at minimum drop the explicit dtype so it matches whatever the framework produces.

Removing the `fill.IsAssignment` guard here assumes that all IBrokerage implementations will only ever raise `OptionPositionAssigned` for actual assignments. Only `BacktestingBrokerage` was updated in this PR. If any live brokerage (e.g., IBKR, Tradier) has the same pre-existing bug—firing `OptionPositionAssigned` for exercises—`_algorithm.OnAssignmentOrderEvent` will now be invoked for exercises, whereas before the guard would have silently swallowed it. Consider either auditing all IBrokerage implementations or retaining a defensive check (with a logged warning) so a contract-violating brokerage doesn't silently corrupt algorithm state.

The behavioral change (only fire `OnOptionPositionAssigned` for assignments, not all exercises) lacks a regression test. The PR checklist acknowledges this. Without a test asserting that (a) assignment fills trigger `OptionPositionAssigned` and (b) exercise fills do not, there is nothing to catch a future regression—especially since the old condition (`order.Type == OrderType.OptionExercise`) was subtly broader and could easily be re-introduced.

Missing `else` before this `if`, causing a double-dispatch bug. When `_isC0Code(wch)` or `_isDelete(wch)` matches and `_ActionIgnore()` is called at lines 1569-1576, execution falls through into this second `if` chain. Since C0 (0x00-0x1F) and DEL (0x7F) match none of the subsequent conditions, they hit the final `else` branch (line 1590-1594) and incorrectly call `_ActionDcsPassThrough(wch)` + `_EnterDcsPassThrough()`. The result is that C0 and DEL characters in the DCS parameter state silently transition the machine into DcsPassThrough instead of being ignored. Fix: change `if` to `else if` on line 1577.

ESC (0x1B) is a C0 code, so `_isC0Code(wch)` is true for it. With the new `_isC0Code(wch) ||` condition, ESC now takes the first branch (`_ActionDcsPassThrough(ESC)`) and never reaches the `else if (_isDcsTerminationInitiator(wch))` branch. This means ESC in the DcsPassThrough state no longer triggers `_EnterDcsTermination()`; instead it is silently passed through as DCS data. The `ProcessCharacter` top-level guard already excludes DcsPassThrough state from the general ESC handler, so `_EventDcsPassThrough` is solely responsible for handling ESC here. Fix: either check `_isDcsTerminationInitiator` before the C0 check, or exclude ESC explicitly from the C0 pass-through path.

Duplicate/misleading test label: cases 16 and 17 both print "Escape from DcsPassThrough", but case 17 sets `mach._state = VTStates::DcsTermination`. The comment should read "Escape from DcsTermination" to reflect the actual state under test.

The added doc comment is too vague for a documentation-improvement PR. 'Might cause unexpected results' doesn't tell the reader what actually happens. Because `remove_from_children` uses `retain(|x| *x != child)`, it removes *all* instances of the child — so a duplicate inserted here will cause the child to be fully removed from the parent rather than just one copy being removed. The doc should state this concrete consequence, e.g.: 'If `child` is already present in `parent`'s [`Children`], a duplicate entry is created. Subsequent calls to remove that child will remove all copies.'

The goroutine closure captures and assigns to the outer `err` variable declared in the enclosing function scope (`_, err = format.ReadFrom(r)`). This is a data race: the goroutine writes `err` while the enclosing function's stack frame owns it. The Go race detector will flag this. Use a local variable instead: `_, localErr := format.ReadFrom(r)`; then assign `formatChErr = localErr`.

A single `format` variable is reused across all iterations and sent by value to the buffered channel. If any field of `rowInDisk` is a slice, each send copies only the slice header—all buffered entries share the same backing array. Because the channel capacity equals `chkSize`, the producer can enqueue all rows before the consumer starts, so every buffered entry will ultimately reflect the last iteration's data (whatever `ReadFrom` wrote last). Either verify that `ReadFrom` always allocates fresh slice storage for each call, or take an explicit copy of `format` before sending (e.g. `cp := format; formatCh <- cp`).

Inside the worker goroutine, `reader.rc.NumChunks()` is called on every loop iteration without holding `reader.rc.m.RLock()`. By contrast, `initializeChannel` acquires that lock for the same call. If chunks are added (or spilled) concurrently—as tested by `TestConcurrentSpillWithRowContainerReader`—this is an unsynchronized read on shared state and a potential data race. Take a snapshot of `NumChunks()` under the read lock before entering the loop, or consistently acquire the lock here.

Duplicate 'image create' event for buildkit builds. This PR also wires `ImageExportedCallback: d` in daemon.go (line 450), which causes `daemon.ImageExportedByBuildkit` (daemon/build.go:14) to fire a create event via the exporter callback. Combined with the `logImageCreateEvent` call here, two `ActionCreate` events are emitted for every buildkit build. One of the two mechanisms should be removed.

`CommitImage` is called for every intermediate build step via `CommitBuildStep` (see comment at line 115), not just for the final image. Adding `LogImageEvent(ActionCreate)` here means a spurious 'create' event is emitted for each intermediate layer during a classic build, flooding the event stream with transient dangling-image events. The create event should only fire for the final committed image.

`break` inside a `select` case exits only the `select`, not the enclosing `for` loop. If `eventsChan` is closed before a matching event arrives, the loop spins tightly on the closed channel (each `time.After` inside the loop is a fresh 3.5 s timer that is always preempted by the immediately-ready closed channel), running indefinitely. Fix: set `finished = true` when `!ok`.

`load_direct_with_reader` uses `loader.default_meta()` but the existing `load_direct` calls `self.asset_server.get_meta_loader_and_reader(&path)`, which reads the actual `.meta` file from disk (including any user-configured settings). By always falling back to default metadata, `load_direct_with_reader` silently ignores custom asset settings (e.g., texture filtering modes, compression formats) that may be defined in `.meta` files for the inner asset path. The PR description says this "mirrors the behaviour of `load_direct`" but this is a material behavioral difference. Consider exposing the reader variant of `get_meta_loader_and_reader` or accepting optional `meta` from the caller.

`uncompressed.take::<A>()` returns `None` if the asset stored inside the `.gz` doesn't actually match type `A` (e.g., the file is a `.gz` of something other than an `Image`). Calling `.unwrap()` on `None` panics the app every frame for that entity, with no recovery path. Since this pattern is the canonical usage shown by the PR, consumers will copy it. Should be replaced with proper error handling, e.g. `match`/`expect` with a descriptive message, or propagating an error through a Result-based system.

Decompression is unbounded: a maliciously crafted or corrupt gzip stream can expand to arbitrary size, exhausting heap memory (zip bomb). While this is an example, it establishes the canonical pattern for production asset loaders. The example should at minimum document this limitation, and the `load_direct_with_reader` API could benefit from a note warning callers to guard against unbounded input before passing a `Reader`.

The variable is named `backspace` but it tracks backslash (`\`) escape state, not backspace (U+0008) characters. This is misleading and will confuse future maintainers. Rename to `backslash` or `escaped`.

If `FormatMessageW` fails it returns 0, making `len` 0 and `msg` an empty string view. The displayed warning becomes "Renderer encountered an unexpected error 0x########: " with nothing after the colon, silently losing the error description. Add a check: `if (!len) { message = ...<fallback>...; break; }` or use `GetLastError()` to explain why `FormatMessageW` itself failed.

When `fontSpec` is a comma-separated list, `FindFontWithLocalizedName` will always return null and the function returns early, skipping the font axes/features refresh that follows. This means the Font Axes and Font Features panels will show stale values whenever the user configures a multi-font fallback list. The TODO comment acknowledges the breakage but the impact is visible to users of the new multi-font feature immediately. Consider at minimum extracting just the primary font name before calling `FindFontWithLocalizedName`, or deferring the axes/features refresh to use `_primaryFontName` directly (which is already correctly set by `AppearanceViewModel`).

`_refreshFontFaceDependents` calls `DWriteCreateFactory` + `GetSystemFontCollection` (with `checkForUpdates=FALSE`) on every font-face change, including during initial construction of each `AppearanceViewModel`. While the factory is shared/cached by DirectWrite, the font collection retrieval still goes through a COM call on every invocation. Consider caching the font collection as a member or retrieving it once in the constructor and reusing it across calls.

The `apply_default_pin_state` field is added to `spi_driver_api` unconditionally, but its sole purpose is to restore PINCTRL-managed pin state. Other optional capabilities in this struct (e.g., `iodev_submit` for `CONFIG_SPI_RTIO`, `begin` for `CONFIG_SPI_ASYNC`) are properly `#ifdef`-guarded. This field, the typedef (line 684), and the public functions (lines 1316–1342) should all be wrapped in `#if defined(CONFIG_PINCTRL)`. Without the guard, every SPI driver across all configurations carries a dead function pointer slot when PINCTRL is disabled.

No SPI driver in the diff implements `apply_default_pin_state`, so all current drivers silently return `-ENOSYS`. Callers (e.g., the ICE40 driver this PR is intended to fix) must treat `-ENOSYS` as a non-fatal condition; if they don't, the bug this PR fixes will simply manifest differently at runtime. The header change alone is incomplete — at least one driver implementation and the corresponding ICE40 call site should be part of this PR to validate the contract.

The filter only checks IFA_F_TENTATIVE but not whether the address is actually link-local (fe80::/10). The purpose of this function (per its name and the log message) is to catch spurious tentative IPv6 link-local addresses, but it will also match tentative global unicast addresses (e.g., from SLAAC or static config during DAD). Those addresses will then be added to addresses_foreign and potentially removed by link_drop_foreign_config. The check should also verify the address falls in the fe80::/10 range using in_addr_is_link_local() before passing it to manager_rtnl_process_address.

Log message says 'Found tentative ipv6 link-local address' but the code does not verify the address is link-local before reaching this point (only tentative is checked). This will produce misleading log output if a non-link-local tentative address is encountered.

The RTM_GETADDR dump is issued with ifindex=0, causing the kernel to return all IPv6 addresses across every interface. The result is then filtered by link->ifindex. On systems with many interfaces/addresses this is wasteful. Check whether sd_rtnl_message_new_addr supports passing link->ifindex directly to scope the dump; if the API requires 0 for dumps, add a comment explaining why.

The error from `url.Parse` is silently discarded. If `u` is malformed, `pu` will be nil, and the returned Remote will have nil FetchURL/PushURL, which will likely cause a nil pointer dereference downstream. Consider propagating the error: `pu, err := url.Parse(u); if err != nil { return nil, err }` (and update the signature to return `(*Remote, error)`).

Direct index `remotes[0]` relies on the invariant that `remotesFunc()` never returns an empty slice without an error. This invariant is currently upheld by the resolver, but it is not type-enforced — a future change to the resolver could silently introduce a panic here. The previous `FindByName` approach returned a typed error when nothing matched; consider keeping that contract or at least adding a guard: `if len(remotes) == 0 { return nil, errors.New("no matching remote") }`.

The closure captures `cachedRemotes` and `remotesError` without any synchronization. If the returned function is ever called concurrently (e.g., from parallel command execution paths), there is a data race on both variables. Protect with a `sync.Mutex` or `sync.Once`.

The test only exercises the happy path of two GHE remotes being preferred when `example.org` is authenticated.
