Missing coverage for critical edge cases: (1) no remotes point to any known host → should return the 'none of the git remotes point to a known GitHub host' error; (2) only a `github.com` remote with no additional config → should succeed and return that remote. These paths contain non-trivial filtering logic that warrants explicit tests.

`xkb_context_set_user_data` and `xkb_context_set_log_fn` are called before the null check on `context`. If `xkb_context_new` returns NULL (e.g. under memory pressure), these calls will dereference NULL and crash. Move the null check to immediately after `xkb_context_new`, before any use of `context`.

Memory leak: if `sway_keyboard_compile_keymap` returns a valid keymap but xkbcommon emitted at least one log message, `error` will be non-NULL and is never freed on the success path. Add `free(error)` after `xkb_keymap_unref(keymap)`.

`length` is `size_t` (result of `vsnprintf(...) + 1`). If the formatted string is empty, `length == 1` and `length - 2` wraps around to `SIZE_MAX - 1`, causing an out-of-bounds read. Guard with `if (length >= 2 && error[length - 2] == '\n')`.

Returns 0 (success) when the port is detected as invalid. This masks the error from callers, who will proceed with a partially-initialised state: `*aclk_hostname` was already allocated with `callocz` at line 187 (memory leak) but `*aclk_port` is never set, leaving the caller with a dangling/NULL pointer. Should return 1 (error) and `freez(*aclk_hostname)` before returning.

The error message claims `https://` is a supported syntax (`http[s]://...`), but `supported_proxy_types[]` only contains an entry for `"http://"` — there is no `https://` entry. The `[s]` is misleading and will confuse users who try an HTTPS proxy URL and receive this message.

Now that HTTP proxies are supported, this config-parse error message only mentions the SOCKS5 syntax. A user who sets `proxy = http://...` in the config will receive an error telling them only SOCKS5 is valid, even though HTTP is also accepted. The message should be updated to list both supported formats.

The lazy-initialisation pattern (read-then-write on static `proxy_type`) is not thread-safe. The comment notes that claiming, challenge/response, and ACLK all call this function, so concurrent first-calls could both pass the `PROXY_NOT_SET` check and both execute `aclk_lws_wss_get_proxy_setting`. A mutex or `pthread_once` should guard the initialisation.

Changing from parallel to sequential connections is a performance regression for all callers of this public API, not just in tests. Users with multiple database connections will now pay N × connection-latency at startup instead of max(latencies). The root cause — Promise.all rejecting early while in-flight connect() calls still complete and register dangling connections — is a test cleanup problem. The proper fix is to ensure the affected test closes its connections in afterAll/afterEach, rather than serializing a production API to mask the symptom.

The comment references an internal test number ('test 8522') rather than documenting the actual technical reason. A reader maintaining this code later won't understand why Promise.all must be avoided without cross-referencing the test suite. Consider replacing with something like: '// Sequential connect prevents dangling connections: if Promise.all rejects early, in-flight connect() calls can still complete after the caller has moved on.'

Missing semicolons on the await statement inside the loop. The rest of the file uses semicolons consistently (e.g., line 90, 95). This is a minor style inconsistency but could trigger linting failures depending on the project's lint rules.

The old hashmap entry is removed unconditionally before the new entry is successfully inserted. If `hashmap_ensure_put` (line 2026) fails with anything other than the handled -EEXIST/bond case — most notably -ENOMEM — the link is completely dropped from `links_by_hw_addr` with no rollback. Subsequent `link_get_by_hw_addr` calls will not find this link, silently reintroducing the false-DAD detection bug for that link. Consider restoring the old entry on failure, or inserting the new entry first before removing the old one.

The bond-slave case is handled by checking `link->kind == "bond"` to decide whether to replace an existing same-MAC entry. However, bond slave interfaces (whose kind is typically not "bond") that are processed before their master will insert successfully, and are then silently replaced when the master registers. Conversely, a slave processed after the master hits -EEXIST without replacement, which is logged as 'Failed to manage link … ignoring'. While this appears to be intentional, the -EEXIST path also swallows any genuine unexpected collision between non-bond, non-slave interfaces, making it harder to diagnose misconfigurations. Consider separating the expected bond-slave -EEXIST case from unexpected ones in the log message.

isPlaying() delegates to ExoPlayer's isPlaying(), which returns false when the player is in STATE_BUFFERING (e.g., poor network on resume). If playback resumes from a buffering state, this condition will be false immediately after playPause() and hideControls() will never be called, leaving the bug unfixed in that scenario. Consider using getPlayWhenReady() instead, which reflects the intent to play synchronously and doesn't depend on the buffering state.

player.hideControls() is called unconditionally, even though player.play() can return early without starting playback (when audioReactor, playQueue, or simpleExoPlayer is null). This would hide controls when the player is not actually playing. Adding a guard (e.g., if (player.getPlayWhenReady())) consistent with the keyboard handler would prevent this.

The `existing_dagruns` pre-flight deduplication check was removed, but no replacement guard was added. `dag.create_dagrun()` is now called unconditionally for every DAG model. Because `generate_run_id(logical_date=None)` appends a random suffix, the `(dag_id, run_id)` unique constraint offers no protection; and because `logical_date=None`, the `(dag_id, logical_date)` unique constraint is also ineffective (SQL NULLs don’t collide). There is no unique constraint on `(dag_id, run_after)` either. In a concurrent HA-scheduler setup, or in any edge case where the `AssetDagRunQueue` row survives (e.g., the row is re-visible after a transaction rollback processed by `@retry_db_transaction`), two dag runs will be silently created for the same asset event. The deleted comment explicitly warned about this: “we need to set dag.next_dagrun_info if the Dag Run already exists or if we create a new one... to avoid a loop of Integrity Error.” A `SELECT … WHERE dag_id=? AND run_after=? AND run_type=ASSET_TRIGGERED` check (similar to the removed `existing_dagruns` bulk check) should be re-added, or `create_dagrun` should be guarded with `on_conflict_do_nothing` / an idempotent upsert.

The `toString()` method includes `secretKey` in plaintext. If this object is ever logged or serialized for debugging, credentials will leak. Mask the value (e.g., `secretKey='***'`) or omit it entirely.

Java `assert` statements are disabled by default at runtime (they require the `-ea` JVM flag). These assertions will silently pass even when the condition is false, making the test meaningless. Use `Assert.assertFalse(flag1)` / `Assert.assertTrue(flag2)` instead. The same issue exists at lines 411 and 418.

The counter is put into the map as an `int` literal (`1`), so it will be stored as `Integer`. Comparing with `2L` (Long) via `assertEquals(Object, Object)` will fail because `Integer(2)` and `Long(2L)` are not equal under `equals()`. Use `Assert.assertEquals(2, version.get(0).get("counter"))` (int) or cast explicitly to match the actual stored type.

Common web font formats are missing from the well-known MIME types list: `.woff` (`font/woff`), `.woff2` (`font/woff2`), `.ttf` (`font/ttf`), and `.ico` (`image/x-icon`). Gitea's UI assets typically include web fonts; if Windows has incorrect registry entries for these types, they'll still be served with wrong MIME types — the exact same class of bug this PR is fixing for `.js`. These should be added to make the fix complete.

`detectWellKnownMimeType` is called redundantly here — `setWellKnownContentType` in `public.go` already ran before `serveContent` was invoked and set the same header. More importantly, this creates a behavioral inconsistency: for extensions not in the well-known list, the gzip path hard-codes `"application/octet-stream"` (line 71), while the non-gzip path (line 80) falls through to `http.ServeContent` which can still attempt content sniffing. A caller that expects content-type sniffing as a fallback for unknown file types will be surprised when gzip is negotiated. Consider removing the redundant detection and relying solely on the pre-set header from `setWellKnownContentType`; if no type was set, `http.ServeContent` handles the gzip reader the same way.

When `TableByID` returns `!ok`, `err` is a stale value from the enclosing function scope (likely `nil` if prior operations succeeded). Assigning it to `b.err` and returning `nil` causes a silent failure: the caller sees a nil executor and nil error and may panic later. Should set a descriptive error, following the established pattern used elsewhere (e.g. line 938: `b.err = errors.Errorf("Can not get table %d", ts.Table.ID)`).

If `partitionIDMap` is nil (i.e. this is a non-global-index IndexLookUpExecutor), any `PartitionHandle` encountered would be silently dropped because a nil-map lookup always returns `false`. In practice `PartitionHandle` is only produced when `e.index.Global` is true (see `getHandle`), so the current code is safe, but the guard is fragile: add a check `if w.idxLookup.partitionIDMap != nil` around the filter to make the intent explicit and protect against future changes to handle-type creation.

`getPartitionIdsAfterPruning` guards against a nil `partInfo` with an error return, but is always called with `&v.PartitionInfo` (address of a struct field), which can never be nil. The nil check is dead code and misleadingly suggests the caller might pass nil. Either remove the guard or change the parameter to a value type `plannercore.PartitionInfo`.

After the reordering, `getPossibleAccessPaths` is now called before the `tableInfo.IsView()` and `tableInfo.IsSequence()` early-return checks. For views and sequences those computed `possiblePaths` are immediately discarded. More importantly, for views `b.optFlag` may already have `flagPartitionProcessor` set from an outer query, causing `hasFlagPartitionProcessor=true` to be passed into `getPossibleAccessPaths` for a view table—this is harmless today (result unused) but fragile. Move the view/sequence checks before `getPossibleAccessPaths`, or skip `getPossibleAccessPaths` for non-data-source table types.

pytest will fail to collect this function because `file` is not a registered fixture. Either rename the function so pytest doesn't try to collect it (e.g., `read_csv_without_encoding`), or define a `file` fixture. As written, running pytest will raise a FixtureLookupError.

Silently swallowing all exceptions and returning them means this function never actually fails — it always "succeeds". The test therefore cannot catch regressions. Raise the exception (or use `pytest.raises`) so failures are surfaced properly.

`writer.writerow(data)` receives a multiline string; the csv writer iterates over it character-by-character, writing each character as a separate column. The commented-out loop suggests the intent was to write rows. Use `data.strip().splitlines()` and iterate with `writer.writerow(row.split(','))` or use `writer.writerows(...)` with proper row data.

`TMP_PATH` is defined but never used. Test files are written to the current working directory (e.g., `test0.csv`) and never cleaned up, polluting the working directory. Use `TMP_PATH` (or a `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory`) and delete files in a finally block.

The error message format `exc?.GetType(): exc?.Message` loses the stack trace compared to `exc.ToString()` used in the equivalent paths in SiloMessageSender and Gateway. The null-safe `?.` operators are also misleading here since `exc` is always non-null when `OnMessageSerializationFailure` is called. Use `exc.ToString()` for consistency and to aid debugging.

Method name typo: `GetFromOtheSilo` is missing the 'r' — should be `GetFromOtherSilo`. Both the interface and the implementation (MessageSerializationGrain.cs line 26) share the typo, so renaming both would fix it without breaking compilation.

`UnserializableType.Deserialize` silently returns `null` instead of throwing. If this deserializer is ever invoked (e.g., if a retry error-response inadvertently routes through this type), the caller receives a null result instead of an exception, creating a hard-to-diagnose test failure. Consider throwing a `NotSupportedException` here to mirror the `Serialize` method's intent and make failures obvious.

The new `is not None` check intends to distinguish `access_control={}` ("clear all") from `access_control=None` ("don't touch"), but this distinction is destroyed before the call reaches here. `DAG._upgrade_outdated_dag_access_control` (dag.py:772) uses `if not access_control: return None`, which converts `{}` to `None`. So `dag.access_control` is always either `None` or a non-empty dict — the explicitly-empty-dict case the PR's design relies on can never arrive here. `_upgrade_outdated_dag_access_control` needs to return `{}` unchanged (instead of `None`) to make the three-way semantic work.

`create_dag_specific_permissions` still uses a truthy check (`if dag.access_control:`) rather than the `is not None` check introduced in `sync_perm_for_dag`. For the new semantics to be consistent across both sync code paths, this should be `if dag.access_control is not None:`. Currently, even after the normalization bug in `_upgrade_outdated_dag_access_control` is fixed, an explicitly-empty `access_control={}` would not trigger a sync through this path.

If `LoadLibraryExW` fails and returns null, `GetProcAddress(nullptr, ...)` is called with an invalid module handle. This overwrites the original `GetLastError()` value from the failed load, so the subsequent `FAIL_FAST_LAST_ERROR_IF(!proc)` on line 20 reports a misleading error code. Add a null check for `module` before (or instead of) relying solely on the `proc` check — e.g., assert `FAIL_FAST_LAST_ERROR_IF(!module)` in the constructor body before the proc check, or use a helper that validates both.

`SystemFunction036` returns BOOLEAN (TRUE = success, FALSE = failure), but the return value is silently discarded. If the call fails, callers of `gen_random` silently receive uninitialised or stale buffer contents as random data. Should propagate or fast-fail on a FALSE return, e.g. `FAIL_FAST_IF(!proc(RandomBuffer, RandomBufferLength))`.

Changing the mock default from `has_temperature_sensor = False` to `True` means every test using `FritzDeviceClimateMock` now exercises only the new primary code path (returning `device.temperature`). The fallback to `device.actual_temperature` — used when `has_temperature_sensor` is False or `temperature` is None — is left untested. A dedicated test with `device.has_temperature_sensor = False` (or `device.temperature = None`) should be added to keep that path covered.

The Fritz!Dect 301 thermostat has its own internal temperature sensor, so `has_temperature_sensor` may already be True even when no external sensor is paired. In scenarios 1 and 2 from the PR description `device.temperature` and `device.actual_temperature` happen to be equal, so this is harmless today. However, if the library ever returns a `temperature` value that diverges from `actual_temperature` for a standalone thermostat (e.g., due to a manual offset being applied differently), the condition would silently pick the wrong value. It is worth verifying that `has_temperature_sensor` is set by the library only when an *external* sensor is paired, or alternatively restructure the condition to directly check for the external-sensor-specific attribute.

Syntax error: there is an unmatched closing parenthesis `)` at the end of the last condition in the boolean expression. The `bool has_invalid_char =` assignment on line 621 has no opening `(`, yet the expression ends with `)`. This will fail to compile. Remove the stray `)` so the line reads `fn.find('|') != std::string::npos;`.

The error message string `invalid_chars` still lists `/` and `\` as forbidden characters, but the new check no longer tests for either of them. This will confuse users who see a validation error listing characters that are not actually checked. The string should be updated to only list the characters that are actually validated: `": * ? < > |`".

The backslash (`\`) check was silently dropped from the Windows invalid-character set. On Windows, `\` is legal as a path separator (and is already handled by the path-resolution code above via `fix_path_separators`), so allowing it through is arguably correct. However the omission is non-obvious and the comment only mentions `/`; at minimum add a brief comment explaining why `\` is intentionally excluded, to prevent future developers from re-adding it or misreading the intent.

arg_tpm2_key_pcr_values is allocated via tpm2_parse_pcr_argument_append (line 520) but has no STATIC_DESTRUCTOR_REGISTER, unlike every other dynamically-allocated arg_* pointer in this file (lines 69-81). Whenever --tpm2-public-key-pcrs is supplied without also triggering the aliasing path, the buffer leaks. Fix: add STATIC_DESTRUCTOR_REGISTER(arg_tpm2_key_pcr_values, freep) alongside the other registrations.

This is a raw pointer alias, not an ownership transfer. After the assignment, both arg_tpm2_hash_pcr_values and arg_tpm2_key_pcr_values point to the same allocation. The STATIC_DESTRUCTOR for arg_tpm2_hash_pcr_values will free it at exit, leaving arg_tpm2_key_pcr_values dangling. Once a destructor is also added for arg_tpm2_key_pcr_values (to fix the leak above), this becomes an immediate double-free. Set arg_tpm2_key_pcr_values = NULL after the assignment to make the ownership transfer explicit and safe.

Silently substituting key PCR values for hash PCR values changes the enrolled TPM2 policy in a way the user did not request. A user who sets --tpm2-pcrs= (empty) intends to skip hash-PCR binding and rely solely on the public-key signature policy. This workaround adds an unintended exact-hash binding on the same PCRs, meaning the secret will unseal only when PCR values exactly match the enrollment-time snapshot—defeating the flexibility that the public-key signature policy is supposed to provide. A narrower fix would be to pass the key PCR set only to the bank-selection logic, not to enroll_tpm2 as the hash PCR set.

The class docstring still says 'The provided datetime MUST be in UTC.' but the fix now silently accepts any timezone-aware datetime and converts it. This is directly contradicted by the new behavior and will mislead future readers. Update the docstring to reflect that timezone-aware datetimes are accepted and automatically converted to UTC.

No test was added for the new timezone-conversion path. The existing test suite covers UTC and naive datetimes but has no case for a non-UTC timezone-aware datetime (e.g. pendulum.now('America/Chicago')), which is exactly the scenario this fix is meant to handle. A test should verify both that the trigger is created successfully and that self.moment is correctly normalized to UTC.

The fix likely does not resolve the root cause. `self.loc(axis=axis)[mask.to_numpy()]` with a boolean numpy array is routed by `_LocIndexer._getitem_axis` into `_getbool_axis` (indexing.py:1511), which does `key.nonzero()` then `self.obj._take(inds, axis=axis)` — the exact same call as the original code. Both paths converge at `_take` with identical integer indices, so if the TypeError originates inside `_take` (likely, given it fires when handling a CategoricalIndex), this change will not fix it. The proper fix should be applied inside `_take` or `CategoricalIndex.take`.

No regression test was added for issue #25087. The PR checklist marks 'tests added / passed', but the commit only modifies frame.py. Without a test covering `dropna()` on a DataFrame with a CategoricalIndex produced by `pd.qcut` (which creates intervals of unequal length), any future regression will go undetected.

The Google Calendar API omits the `items` key entirely when no events match the query (rather than returning an empty list). Using `response["items"]` will raise a `KeyError` in that case. Use `response.get("items", [])` instead.

The string `'false'` is not a valid value for the `sendUpdates` API parameter. The API accepts `"all"`, `"externalOnly"`, or `"none"`. Passing `'false'` will result in an API error for any `create_event` call that uses the default. Change the default to `"none"`.

The Google Calendar API expects RFC 3339 formatted strings (e.g. `"2021-01-01T00:00:00Z"`) for `timeMax`, `timeMin`, and `updatedMin`. Passing raw Python `datetime` objects will produce a non-compliant string representation (space separator instead of `T`) when the HTTP client serializes the query parameter, causing API errors. Convert them before passing: `time_max.isoformat()` (ensure timezone-awareness), or change the type hint to `str` and document the expected format.

The comment says the crash only affects 'Adreno GPU chipsets', but `#[cfg(feature = "webgl")]` applies to all WebGL targets. Non-Adreno WebGL users (e.g. Mali-G78, desktop browsers) who don't experience this crash are also silently limited to 1 directional light, which is an unannounced regression. Either the fix should be more targeted (e.g. a runtime check on the adapter name/vendor ID) or the comment should be corrected to reflect that this is a limitation for all WebGL builds, not just Adreno.

The comment attributes the crash specifically to `apply_fog` in `pbr_functions.wgsl`, but `MAX_DIRECTIONAL_LIGHTS` controls the entire directional-light uniform buffer array, which affects lighting broadly (not just fog). The actual root cause—whether it is a uniform buffer size limit, array stride issue, or something else in the Adreno/WebGL driver—is not described. A more precise explanation would help future maintainers understand when this workaround can be revisited or removed.

Adding `setArgs` to the `builtinFunc` interface exposes a raw argument-mutation method to all consumers of the interface, bypassing the validation and type-coercion that normally happens through `NewFunctionInternal`. This method is only needed inside `EvaluateExprWithNull`. A safer approach is to keep `setArgs` as an unexported method on `baseBuiltinFunc` only and use a package-local type assertion (or a narrower internal interface) in the one call site, rather than widening the public interface.

After `Clone()` + `setArgs`, the cloned expression keeps the original `RetType` without recalculation. `NewFunctionInternal` would have re-derived the return type (including nullability flags) based on the new arguments. For the null-rejection test in `EvaluateExprWithNull` this may not matter today, but if callers ever rely on the returned expression's `RetType` being accurate for the null-substituted args, this silent type mismatch could cause subtle downstream bugs. At minimum, a comment explaining why `RetType` staleness is acceptable here would help future readers.

Hold-to-repeat tab switching is broken. The old code kept `keyReleased = true` specifically for `SwapTabLeft`/`SwapTabRight`, enabling repeated firing while holding Ctrl+Tab. The new blanket `keyReleased = false` prevents this for the replacement `PreviousTab`/`NextTab` commands. The PR description explicitly references issue #11737 ('hold down Ctrl+Tab to switch more than once') as a desired behavior. Fix: `keyReleased = command.Code is CommandCodes.PreviousTab or CommandCodes.NextTab;`

`IsExecutable` permits execution when `TabCount` is 1 (the only tab open). All other navigation-adjacent tab actions use `TabCount > 1`. If the app does not gracefully handle closing the final tab (e.g. opening a blank tab or closing the window), this can leave the user in a broken state. Verify the `CloseTab` call on the control handles the last-tab case, or add a guard like `context.TabCount > 1` if closing the last tab should be disallowed.

The test data uses unique values for 'c' (1, 2, 3), so every group has exactly one row. This means each rolling window can only ever produce a single-element list, which is the trivial case. A group with multiple rows—where the rolling window spans more than one element—should also be tested to verify that multi-element lists are correctly preserved rather than auto-exploded.

The fix lives in the shared `finish_rolling` helper and therefore affects all `rolling_*` functions (rolling_min, rolling_max, rolling_sum, rolling_std, rolling_var, etc.), but the test only exercises `rolling_mean`. At minimum one additional rolling variant with a `by=` argument should be tested to guard against per-function regressions.

The route `POST /indexes/:uid/documents` is missing from the test array. This is the primary motivating case from the issue (it previously inferred JSON without enforcing Content-Type), yet it has no coverage here. Add "/indexes/doggo/documents" to the routes list.

The issue's TODO lists `PUT indexes/:uid` and `PUT indexes/:uid/documents` as routes that should enforce Content-Type, but neither is tested. The current loop only issues POST requests (via `TestRequest::post()`), so these PUT routes need a separate test block.
`GetAllDocumentsOptions` is imported but never used in this file. This will produce a compiler warning (`unused import`). Remove it from the use statement.

This assertion has no failure message, unlike the equivalent checks in the outer loop. When it fails, there is no indication of which route or content-type triggered it. Add a message: `assert_eq!(status_code, 415, "route `{}` with content-type `{}` should return 415", route, bad_content_type)`.

The log message reports `finalFile.FullName` (the write destination) but the file being read is `processedFile.FullName`. When the two paths differ this log entry will be actively misleading during debugging. Change the interpolated path to `processedFile.FullName` to accurately reflect what is being opened.

The old code fell back to reading `finalFile` if it existed, preserving any data already written to the destination. Now only `processedFile` is checked; if `finalFile` exists in the destination but `processedFile` does not (e.g., during a first run after migration where the two directories differ), its existing contents are silently overwritten rather than merged. Consider also checking `finalFile.Exists` as a fallback when `processedFile` is absent, or at least document and log that scenario explicitly.

The XML doc `<param>` tags still reference the old parameter names `sourceFile` and `destinationFile`, and there is no entry for the new `processedFilesDirectory` parameter. This makes auto-generated docs and IDE tooltips incorrect.

Index raises TypeError immediately while Series/DTA only emits a FutureWarning for the same operation. This breaks existing Index code without a deprecation period, which is inconsistent with the PR's stated goal of deprecation (not immediate removal). The Index branch should emit a FutureWarning here and then fall through to `values.astype(dtype)` (which calls DTA.astype and warns again), or match the DTA.astype warning approach rather than raising outright.
Error message does not include the offending dtype, making it difficult to diagnose. Should be: `f"Cannot cast {type(self).__name__} to dtype {dtype}"`

Comment `# unit conversion e.g. datetime64[s]` is copy-pasted from the non-unitless branch above and is misleading here — this branch handles *unitless* datetime64 (no unit at all), not a unit conversion like datetime64[s]. Should say something like `# unitless datetime64, e.g. np.datetime64`.

`copy` parameter is silently ignored in this deprecated branch — `self._ndarray.astype(dtype)` always copies. Callers passing `copy=False` will silently get a copy instead of a possible view. This is the same behavior as the original pre-regression code, but since the branch now also emits a deprecation warning it is worth documenting (or passing `copy=copy` to numpy's astype, which at minimum honors the intent).

The null guard `if (_request is null) return false;` already existed in the pre-change code (visible as the removed `-` line in the diff). This PR only reformats it by adding curly braces — no new null check is introduced. If the NullReferenceException described in the issue is real, the root cause is not addressed here. The bug may live elsewhere (e.g., the `ContentType` property accesses `_request.ContentType` without a null guard, though that path appears to be guarded by the `if (Form != null)` early return in `HasFormContentType`). The fix and its description need to be reconciled.

The <param> tags are nested inside the <summary> block (the closing </summary> is on line 66, after the params). XML doc tooling (DocFX, Roslyn, IntelliSense) expects <param> elements to appear as siblings of <summary>, not children. The </summary> should be moved to immediately after line 62, and the three <param> lines should follow it.

The fenced code block opened with ` ```python ` on this line is never closed before the `## Non-fluent Call Expressions` heading on line 321. In rustdoc, everything from line 315 onward (the heading and prose) would be rendered as raw code rather than markdown. Add a closing ` ``` ` after line 319 (`/// )`).

Dangling sentence fragment left over from editing. Line 324 ends with `...if the \`callee\` expression and the opening parentheses fit.` and then line 325 adds `/// fits on the line.` – this is a duplicate trailing phrase that should be removed.
Typo in comment: "don't loos the comment" should be "don't lose the comment".

For single-target assignments where the target has its own parentheses (the `else if` branch), `RightToLeft` is used with the target as `before_operator`. Inside `RightToLeft`, if both `should_inline_comments` and `should_non_inlineable_use_best_fit` are false for the value (e.g. an attribute expression whose `needs_parentheses` returns `Multiline` or `Never`), the code falls through to `maybe_parenthesize_expression(..., Parenthesize::IfBreaks)`. This effectively skips the right-to-left layout entirely for such values, quietly reverting to left-to-right behavior. Whether this is intentional should be documented; a non-obvious category of expressions (non-BestFit attributes/calls) silently bypasses the preview logic.

The change removes the `memdup` so `c.data` now points directly into the caller's buffer rather than an owned copy. This is safe for the current `hashmap_remove` lookup (the pointer is only read, not stored), but `DHCPClientId` elsewhere carries ownership of its `data` field (allocated via `memdup` on insertion). If a cleanup helper for `DHCPClientId` is ever introduced or called on `c` in future refactoring, this non-owning pointer would cause a double-free or use-after-free. A comment noting that `c.data` is intentionally non-owning here (lookup key only) would make the invariant explicit and prevent accidental breakage.

The diff does not appear to address the stated issue. The bug report and PR title both describe Windows RNDIS clients being dropped because the server rejects packets whose `htype` is not `ARPHRD_ETHER` (0x01). The fix should be near the `htype` validation in the packet-receive path. The change here only removes a `memdup` in the unrelated static-lease deletion path and has no effect on how incoming DHCP packets are filtered by hardware type.

Logger cleanup is not in a teardown or `ensure` block. If the assertion on line 149 fails, `MyLogSubscriber.logger` stays set to the custom `MockLogger`, potentially causing other tests to behave incorrectly. Move the cleanup to the class `teardown` method or wrap the test body in `ensure`.

Removing memoization from the base `LogSubscriber.logger` means `Rails.logger` is called on every log event (SQL queries, controller actions, etc.) instead of once. The old `@logger ||=` only skipped caching when `Rails.logger` was nil; the new code never caches. Consider keeping memoization on the base class (where inheritance delegation is not needed) or memoizing only when a non-nil value is returned.

`return @logger if @logger` uses truthiness rather than `instance_variable_defined?(:@logger)`. This means setting a subclass logger to `nil` is indistinguishable from never having set it — it silently falls back to the superclass logger instead of returning `nil`. Users who want a subclass to suppress logging by setting its logger to `nil` will be surprised. Using `instance_variable_defined?` (and removing the ivar to 'reset') would make the contract explicit.

The FQ structs don't derive `Clone` or `Copy`. Because `quote!` requires non-iterator values used inside `#(...)*` repetitions to implement `Clone`, callers are forced to call `.into_token_stream()` as a workaround before using them in repetitions (e.g. `structs.rs:10`, `tuple_structs.rs:10`, `from_reflect.rs:38,78`). This inconsistency is a maintenance trap: future contributors could use `#FQOption` directly inside a repetition and get a confusing compile error. Adding `#[derive(Clone, Copy)]` to all FQ structs would eliminate these workarounds and make the API safe to use uniformly.

`FQBox` expands to `::std::boxed::Box` while every other FQ type uses `::core::*` paths. If this crate ever targets `no_std + alloc`, `FQBox` would be the only one that breaks. Prefer `::alloc::boxed::Box` for consistency and forward-compatibility.

The overflow guard `(int64_t)(to->dticks - ticks_elapsed) > (int64_t)INT_MAX` is dead code on 32-bit platforms. The subtraction is evaluated in `int32_t` before the cast, so the result is at most `INT32_MAX` == `INT_MAX` and the condition can never be true. If the intent is to detect when the remaining ticks would overflow `int32_t`, the cast must happen before the subtraction: `((int64_t)to->dticks - (int64_t)ticks_elapsed) > INT32_MAX`. As written, the only meaningful branch condition is `to == NULL`.

Memory leak when the node is already in the blacklist. `dictAddOrFind` returns the existing entry without consuming the newly allocated `id` key, so `id` is never freed. The fix should check whether a new entry was inserted (i.e. whether `dictAddRaw` added the key) and call `sdsfree(id)` if the key already existed. One clean approach: use `dictAddRaw` with an `existing` out-param, free `id` when `existing != NULL`, and update the expiry on the returned entry.

The test drops table `t` but creates `t1` and `t2`. If `t1` or `t2` already exist from a previous test run (e.g. due to a failed teardown), `CREATE TABLE t1` will error out. Should be `drop table if exists t1, t2` to properly clean up the tables actually used by this test.

Blocking all physical PointGet conversion whenever `UseCache` is true is overly broad. This prevents caching of legitimate PointGet plans (e.g., `where pk = 5`) that are safe to cache, simply because the statement is a prepared one. The fix should only suppress conversion when the ranges were derived from mutable/parameterised constants that could produce a PointGet only for specific parameter values.

When a mutable constant is detected for one column, returning `nil, conditions, nil, nil, false` abandons eq/in extraction for *all* columns in the index, not just the mutable one. Conditions on other, non-mutable columns could still be extracted to form a tighter index range. The early exit may significantly degrade index usage for composite indexes where only some columns have parameter markers.

When `git merge --ff-only` fails because the PR branch has diverged from the base (the expected failure mode for this style), git outputs "fatal: Not possible to fast-forward, aborting." without creating a MERGE_HEAD file. `runMergeCommand` only recognises two specific failure patterns (MERGE_HEAD existence and 'refusing to merge unrelated histories'); this case falls through to the generic `fmt.Errorf` path, which is unhandled in the web router and becomes a 500 Server Error instead of a user-facing 'sync your branch first' message. Add a check in `runMergeCommand` (or in this function before returning the error) for the 'Not possible to fast-forward' stderr string and surface it as a distinct, actionable error.

Auto-merge is allowed for fast-forward-only (`hideAutoMerge: generalHideAutoMerge`). If a user schedules auto-merge while the PR is up-to-date, then the base branch is pushed to before auto-merge fires, the `--ff-only` operation will fail. Because of the missing error handling noted above, this failure surfaces as a 500 rather than a clear message. Unlike other merge styles, FF-only has a hard precondition (no commits behind) that can be invalidated asynchronously after scheduling. Consider setting `hideAutoMerge: true` here (matching the `manually-merged` style) or ensuring the failure path is handled gracefully first.

`AllowFastForwardOnly: true` is added to the default config for every new repository, enabling FF-only for all repos out of the box. Every other existing style (merge, rebase, squash) is also enabled, so this is internally consistent, but FF-only is strictly more restrictive (it refuses to merge if the head branch is behind the base). Enabling it by default may surprise repository owners who have not opted into a strict-linear-history policy; consider defaulting it to `false` (matching the `AllowManualMerge: false` precedent for opt-in styles), or gating it on a new `app.ini` setting like the other styles.

The override comment says 'Dangling comments are handled in `fmt_fields`', but `fmt_fields` no longer contains any dangling-comment emission (the old `dangling_node_comments(parameters.as_ref())` call was removed). For the `parameters = Some(...)` case, dangling comments are now emitted by `FormatParameters` (via `dangling_comments(dangling)` in the `ParametersParentheses::Never` branch); for the `parameters = None` case, the assumption is that no comments end up as dangling on the lambda node. The comment should be updated to reflect this, and ideally a `debug_assert!(dangling_comments.is_empty())` added to catch any future mis-categorised comments that would otherwise be silently dropped.

The doc comment still reads 'The arguments to any lambdas within the current lambda body', but the field type is now `Vec<Option<&'a Parameters>>`, where `None` represents a nested parameter-less lambda. The description should note that `None` entries represent lambdas with no parameters, to clarify why the check uses `is_some_and` rather than a direct `.includes()` call.

`are_parameters_parenthesized` uses a source-text heuristic (`starts_with('(')`) to distinguish function-definition parameters (always parenthesized) from lambda parameters (never parenthesized). This is correct for all valid Python 3 syntax, but it is fragile: it relies on the `parameters.range` including the opening paren for function definitions and not starting with `(` for lambdas. If range computation ever changes (e.g. for generated or partially-synthetic nodes), this would silently misclassify. A structural check—or at least an assertion that the range is non-empty before slicing—would be safer.

MockClientWriter<W> uses W as the *write* type (client-side requests), so this should be MockClientWriter<EchoRequest>, not EchoResponse. Same semantic mistake appears for MockClientReaderWriter (line 38, first type param) and MockClientAsyncWriter (line 50). While compilation succeeds with any proto type, the mismatch makes this a misleading usage example and could confuse readers of the test.

MockClientReaderWriter<W, R>: W is the write (request) type and R is the read (response) type. Using <EchoResponse, EchoResponse> has the wrong write type; it should be <EchoRequest, EchoResponse>. Note that MockClientAsyncReaderWriter on line 53-54 gets this right.
MockClientAsyncWriter<W> uses W as the write (request) type; this should be MockClientAsyncWriter<EchoRequest>, not EchoResponse.

All ASSERT_NE(ptr, nullptr) checks are vacuous: absl::make_unique never returns null (it throws std::bad_alloc on failure). The test's actual value is purely in proving the mocks are concrete types (compilation check). Consider adding at least one EXPECT_CALL / invocation per mock to give the test real runtime value, or add a comment noting it is an intentional compilation-only test.

Guest no longer inherits from Vagrant.plugin("2", :guest) (previously via GuestLinux::Guest). While detect? works via duck typing and plugin.rb still registers :linux as the capability parent, the class has no Vagrant plugin ancestry. If any Vagrant internals call inherited methods or perform class-ancestry checks, this will fail unexpectedly. Consider inheriting directly from Vagrant.plugin("2", :guest) to preserve the proper plugin contract.

`source` is a bashism not guaranteed to exist in BusyBox ash (OpenWrt's default shell). The POSIX-compliant form is `. /etc/os-release`. If `source` is unavailable, this branch silently fails and the os-release detection path becomes dead code. The /etc/openwrt_release check above still provides a primary detection path, but this branch should use `. /etc/os-release` for reliability.

Missing `stacklevel` in `warnings.warn`. The default stacklevel=1 points the warning at this library line rather than the caller's code. Use `stacklevel=2` (or higher if there are additional call layers) so users see which of their own lines triggered the deprecation. This applies to the same pattern in all five changed files.

When a caller uses the new `token` argument (not `use_auth_token`), the code re-injects it as `kwargs["use_auth_token"] = token`. If the downstream `from_pretrained` implementations also emit a deprecation warning upon seeing `use_auth_token`, callers who correctly use the new API will still see a spurious deprecation warning. Consider suppressing that downstream warning (e.g. via `warnings.catch_warnings`) or checking whether the downstream layer has already been updated to accept `token` directly. This applies identically to all five changed files.

Nil pointer dereference: `t.SessionID.Load()` is called before the `if t != nil` guard. If `MemUsageTop1Tracker.Load()` returns nil, this will panic. Move `sessionID := t.SessionID.Load()` inside the `if t != nil` block.

If `err1` is nil (kill does not trigger as expected), calling `err1.Error()` will panic rather than produce a meaningful test failure. Use `require.ErrorContains(t, err1, ...)` instead, which safely handles a nil error.

`S_FALSE` is returned when no suitable reference glyph is found, but `*effect` is left uninitialized. `SUCCEEDED(S_FALSE)` evaluates to `true`, so callers that check only `SUCCEEDED(hr)` will proceed to use an uninitialized (or null) effect pointer, risking a null dereference. Either document that callers must check `hr == S_OK` (not just `SUCCEEDED`), or initialize `*effect = nullptr` before the early return so callers can safely check for null.

No guard against `boxHeightDesignUnits` being zero (or negative, if both bearings exceed the advance height for a malformed font). Dividing by zero produces an `inf` or `NaN` scale factor, which will corrupt the D2D transform matrix for all subsequent glyph draws in the run. Add a check `if (boxHeightDesignUnits > 0)` before computing the scale factor, or return `S_OK` with the default 1.0 factor if the glyph metrics are degenerate. The same issue exists for `boxWidthDesignUnits` on the horizontal path (~line 1502).

The horizontal 'cell' bounds are set to `boxCenter` on both sides, so `cellWidthDesignUnits == advanceWidth`. This implicitly assumes the font's advance width exactly equals the terminal's physical cell width. Unlike the vertical path, which derives cell dimensions from `format->GetLineSpacing()` (the actual pixel-snapped cell height), the horizontal path has no equivalent correction for the rounding/padding the terminal applies when sizing cells. If the two differ, the horizontal scale factor will be off by a constant factor across all box-drawing glyphs, potentially leaving small gaps or overlaps between adjacent cells.

The removed line `_selectionBackground = D2D1::ColorF(D2D1::ColorF::Red);` was unconditionally overwriting the correctly computed selection color with solid red immediately after setting it. This was a debug artifact that made all selection highlights appear red in production. Its removal is correct, but confirms a latent rendering bug that existed in the previously-shipped code.

Calling the private `_async_refresh` method to exploit the `log_failures=False` parameter is fragile. This bypasses the public API contract; if HA's coordinator internals change, this will break silently. A more robust approach would be to call the public `async_refresh()` inside a try/except and handle the failure there, or check API availability before constructing the coordinator.

`except RequestException` catches all network-level errors (timeouts, DNS failures, 5xx server errors, etc.), not only 'location not in covered zone' errors. A transient network failure during setup will permanently suppress the rain coordinator (since `last_update_success` stays False and the coordinator is never stored). The fix conflates transient vs. permanent failures — only HTTP 4xx 'not found / outside coverage' errors should cause the sensor to be silently skipped; other errors should propagate or trigger a retry.

`coordinator_rain` is always a `DataUpdateCoordinator` instance at this point (assigned unconditionally at line 80, never reassigned to `None` afterward). The `coordinator_rain and` truthiness check is dead code and misleadingly implies it could be `None` here. Only `coordinator_rain.last_update_success` is meaningful.

Using `assert` for runtime validation is unsafe. Python's `-O` (optimize) flag disables all `assert` statements, so this check would be silently skipped in optimized environments, leading to incorrect tensor indexing instead of a clear error. Replace with an explicit `if` + `raise ValueError`.

The docstring states labels should be `torch.LongTensor`, but when `num_labels == 1` the code uses `MSELoss`, which requires float inputs. Passing a `LongTensor` to `MSELoss` will raise a runtime error. The docstring (and ideally a `.float()` cast on the labels in the regression branch) should reflect this distinction.
Debug `print` statement left in the test method. Should be removed before merging.

When the fallback `remove_one_by_one` itself fails (e.g. `NoSuchEntity`), the failure is only logged at `debug` level. A user watching warn-level logs will see the initial 'falling back to one-by-one' warning but never learn that the fallback also failed completely and no components were removed. Consider logging this at `warn` as well, since it signals a total failure of the remove operation.

The entity's metadata (`loc.archetype` and `loc.index`) is updated in safe code between the two unsafe calls: after `target_arch.allocate` but before `source_arch.move_to` completes. If the `move_to` closure panics (e.g. via the `ptr::copy_nonoverlapping` or the subsequent `unwrap`), the entity location now points to the new archetype but none of the component data has been moved, leaving the world in a corrupt state. This ordering is unchanged from before the refactoring, but the split into two separate `unsafe` blocks makes it more apparent. Adding a comment acknowledging the invariant (or using `catch_unwind` to restore the old location on panic) would improve robustness.

The `Tuple` (and `List`) arms recurse into elements, which is correct for regular assignment (`(a, b) = [1, 2]`) but wrong for augmented assignment. `(a, b) += 5` is a Python `SyntaxError` ("'tuple' is an illegal expression for augmented assignment"), yet the current code allows it through because each element (`a`, `b`) is a valid `Name`. A separate validation function (e.g., `invalid::augmented_assignment_target`) should reject `Tuple`, `List`, and `Starred` at the top level, and be called from the `AugAssign` rule in `python.lalrpop` instead of the shared `assignment_target`.

The call site for augmented assignment validation. Because `invalid::assignment_target` is reused here, tuple and list targets like `(a, b) += 5` or `[a, b] += 5` are not rejected even though CPython raises `SyntaxError` for them. This is the companion fix location: replacing or wrapping this call with one that additionally rejects `Tuple`, `List`, and `Starred` targets.

`Slice` nodes cannot appear as a top-level assignment target in this grammar (they only occur inside `Subscript` nodes), so this arm is likely dead code. It is harmless, but could mislead future readers into thinking bare slice expressions are a concern here.

`SampleMethodTypeNone` (the zero value of the SampleMethod enum) is removed from the allowed set, so any `TableSample` node whose `SampleMethod` field was never explicitly set will now silently hit the `default` error branch instead of passing through. This is correct for `TABLESAMPLE ()` from the parser, but if any internal code constructs a `*ast.TableSample` without setting `SampleMethod` (relying on the previous zero-value pass-through), it will start returning `ErrInvalidTableSample`. Worth confirming no other code path depends on the old zero-value behavior.

The expression `(uintptr_t) (p)-1` is parsed as `((uintptr_t)(p)) - 1` due to C operator precedence (cast binds tighter than subtraction), but it reads ambiguously — a reader could mistake it for `(uintptr_t)((p)-1)`. Adding explicit parentheses (`((uintptr_t)(p) - 1)`) makes the intent unambiguous and avoids subtle misreads in a security-sensitive macro.

`MODE_INVALID` has been moved from `parse-util.h` to `fs-util.h`. Any translation unit that relied on picking up `MODE_INVALID` transitively through `parse-util.h` (without directly including `fs-util.h`) will now fail to compile. Given that `parse-util.h` declares `parse_mode()` which is closely related to `mode_t`, callers of `parse_mode` are likely candidates for using `MODE_INVALID` — verify all such callers include `fs-util.h`.

Data race: `Clone()` reads `c.RetType` (the pointer) without synchronization, while `GetType()` writes `c.RetType` inside `once.Do`. `sync.Once` only guarantees the closure runs once — it does NOT protect concurrent reads of `c.RetType` from other goroutines. The existing comment on `ParamMarker` explicitly notes that `GetType()` can be called in a multi-threaded context (e.g., building inner executors of IndexJoin), so this race is realistic. A read-write mutex around `RetType` access, or eagerly computing the flag at construction time, would be safer.

For `Constant` nodes with `DeferredExpr` set, `c.Value` reflects the plan-time evaluation, not the execution-time result. If `c.Value` is non-null at plan time, `once.Do` will permanently mark `RetType` with `NotNullFlag`. However, when `DeferredExpr` is a non-deterministic function it may return null at execution time, making the `NotNullFlag` inaccurate. The `ParamMarker` path already has an early return to avoid this kind of stale inference; a similar guard (`if c.DeferredExpr != nil { return c.RetType }`) before the `once.Do` block would prevent incorrectly marking deferred-expression constants as NOT_NULL.

`RetType` is a public field. Any code that reads `constant.RetType` directly — rather than calling `constant.GetType()` — will miss the lazily-applied `NotNullFlag`, creating an invisible invariant that is easy to violate. Consider making the field unexported (with a public accessor) or setting the flag eagerly at construction time so the field value is always authoritative.

Using a dummy string `"sgd"` as a workaround is fragile and slightly misleading. The cleaner fix is to not pass an optimizer at all when not training: call `model.compile(metrics=metrics)` (without the `optimizer=` argument) in the else-branch, so Keras uses its own default. This avoids initializing an SGD optimizer that will never be used and removes the need for the 'write anything' comment. The same pattern applies to the other 6 scripts changed in this PR.

The comment here (`# Just use any default`) is inconsistent with all other files in this PR, which use `# Just write anything because we won't be using it`. Consistency helps future readers understand the shared intent.

Unlike every other file in this PR—where `optimizer` is assigned to a variable and then passed to `model.compile(optimizer=optimizer, ...)`—here `"sgd"` is hardcoded directly in the `compile()` call. The inconsistency makes it harder to apply a follow-up fix uniformly if needed. Prefer assigning `optimizer = "sgd"` separately, matching the pattern used in the other scripts.

Function is named `shell_log_backend_suspend` but the header declares `z_shell_log_backend_suspend`. The caller in smp_shell.c calls `z_shell_log_backend_suspend`, so this will cause a linker error. Rename to `z_shell_log_backend_suspend`.

`z_shell_log_backend_resume` is called unconditionally every time a non-mcumgr byte is received, even if the backend was never suspended (e.g., normal shell input with no prior TX). This will force the backend to ENABLED and silently undo any deliberate `z_shell_log_backend_disable` call. Add a flag or check the current state before resuming.

`shell_backend_uart_get_ptr()` hardcodes the UART shell backend. If the SMP shell is running over a different backend (e.g., RTT), this returns the wrong shell pointer or NULL, leading to a NULL dereference. The shell instance should be passed in or retrieved from the transport context rather than assumed to be the UART backend.

`z_shell_log_backend_resume` sets state directly to `SHELL_LOG_BACKEND_ENABLED` without checking whether the backend was previously enabled (vs. disabled via `z_shell_log_backend_disable`). Resuming should restore the prior state, not unconditionally enable, to avoid activating a backend that was intentionally disabled before the suspend.

The string literal `"skippedArgs"` creates hidden coupling to the field name. If `skippedArgs` is renamed during refactoring, this throws `IllegalStateException` at class-load time with no compile-time indication that the string needs updating. IDE rename refactorings typically don't update reflection string literals. Consider a compile-time check (e.g., verifying the annotation is present on the returned field) or at least add a comment warning that the field name and string literal must be kept in sync.

Unchecked cast `(List<String>) value.getValue()` suppresses the compiler's type-safety check. The cast is safe by construction (a `allowMultiple=true` `String`-typed option always stores a `List<String>`), but the generated compiler warning is a sign that this should be documented. Add `@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")` with a brief comment explaining why the cast is safe.

`@VisibleForTesting` signals that a method's visibility was widened solely for test access. However, `parseGivenArgs` is called directly from the production `parse()` method (line 134), making it part of the production call chain, not just a test hook. Future maintainers may incorrectly infer it is safe to delete or restrict this method when all test references are removed. Consider removing the annotation or replacing it with a comment that explains its dual role.

The promise `new Promise(f => readable.on('readable', f))` has no error handling. If `fs.createReadStream` emits an 'error' event before 'readable' (e.g., file not found, permission denied), the promise never settles. The surrounding try/catch does not help here because the error is asynchronous. This causes the entire `saveAsStream` call to hang indefinitely. Fix: also listen for the 'error' event and reject, e.g. `new Promise((f, r) => { readable.on('readable', f); readable.on('error', r); })`.

Same hang risk as in `saveAsStream`: `await new Promise(f => readable.on('readable', f))` in `stream()` has no error handler. An 'error' event on the readable stream (e.g., file gone between `localPathAfterFinished` and `createReadStream`) would leave this promise permanently pending.

`_finishedCallback()` (which resolves `_finishedPromise`) is only reached if all save callbacks succeed. If any awaited callback in the `for` loop at lines 107–111 rejects, `_finishedCallback()` is never called, leaving `_finishedPromise` permanently pending. Everything awaiting `finishedPromise()` or `localPathAfterFinished()` will then hang forever. Wrap the callback loop in a try/finally to guarantee `_finishedCallback()` always runs.

`saveCallback(this._localPath).catch(e => {})` silently discards any rejection from the callback. In `ArtifactDispatcher.saveAs` the callback is an async function that calls the outer `resolve`/`reject`; if it were to throw before doing so (e.g., due to a future code change), the dispatcher's promise would hang with no error surfaced. Consider propagating the error rather than swallowing it.

The `video` channel event is subscribed to but the handler is entirely empty (the `artifact` parameter is unused). Registering a no-op listener for an unimplemented feature adds noise. Either remove the subscription until it is needed, or assign the artifact to a context-level collection so that video objects can be surfaced via the BrowserContext API as intended.

Circular import risk: `pandas/core/indexes/base.py` is a foundational module imported early during pandas initialization. Adding `import pandas as pd` here can cause a circular import (pandas -> indexes -> base -> pandas). The specific symbol needed (`pd.DataFrame`) should either be imported locally inside the function or avoided entirely (see the return-type issue below).

This `except KeyError` block is unreachable dead code. The preceding `except KeyError as err` clause (line 3792) already handles all `KeyError` exceptions raised in the `try` block. Python matches the first applicable `except` clause, so this second `except KeyError` will never execute.

`self.index` does not exist on `Index` objects — `self` IS the index. This should be `self.is_monotonic_increasing`. As written, if this code were ever reached it would raise `AttributeError` instead of the intended warning.

`Index.get_loc()` must return an `int`, `slice`, or boolean `np.ndarray` — callers rely on this contract (e.g. for fancy indexing). Returning `pd.DataFrame()` here will cause `TypeError` or silent data corruption downstream in any code path that calls `get_loc` and then uses the result to index into an array or Series.

`warnings.warn` is missing a `stacklevel` argument. Without it the warning points into pandas internals rather than the user's call site. Use `stacklevel=find_stack_level()` (already used elsewhere in this file) so the warning is attributed to the user's `.loc[...]` line.

wlr_seat_touch_notify_up is called AFTER seatop_begin_default, reversing the expected event ordering. seatop_begin_default calls seatop_end (freeing the old state) and then rebases pointer focus, which can send pointer-enter/motion events to clients before they receive the touch-up. The notify_up call should come before the seatop transition so clients see: touch_up → pointer_enter, not the reverse.

If found is false and sway_assert does not abort (e.g., in NDEBUG release builds), execution continues with point_event pointing to the wl_list sentinel embedded in seatop_down_event, causing undefined behaviour when its fields are read. A concrete path to this: a second touch_down that lands on no surface returns early at line 79-81 without adding an entry to point_events, and then a touch_motion for that same ID reaches this function. The guard should be 'if (!found) { return; }' rather than an assert-and-continue.

seat->touch_x/touch_y are now updated unconditionally for every touch_id, not just the primary (seat->touch_id). After a secondary-touch motion event, these fields no longer reflect the primary touch position. Code like handle_touch_down in seatop_down.c that calls node_at_coords(seat, seat->touch_x, seat->touch_y, ...) would read a stale secondary-touch position if a new finger-down occurs between two primary-touch motion events. Consider only updating these fields when event->touch_id == seat->touch_id, and passing lx/ly explicitly to seatop_touch_motion instead.

Using column name as the hash key means that when a query returns multiple columns with the same name (e.g., two `COALESCE(...)` expressions without aliases, both yielding the name `coalesce`), each iteration overwrites the previous entry. The last column's type wins for every column sharing that name, so earlier columns get cast with the wrong type. The test change in `calculations_test.rb` explicitly documents this regression: integer id values are now returned as strings for such queries on PostgreSQL. Consider keying `types` by column index instead of name, or at least documenting that the fix degrades type-casting accuracy for queries with duplicate column names.

The test now accepts strings (`"1"`, `"2"`, …) instead of integers for the first column on PostgreSQL, documenting a behavioral regression introduced by this fix. The comment describes it as a known limitation and tells users to use aliases as a workaround, but this silently breaks existing callers who use `pluck` with unaliased SQL functions. It would be worth either fixing the root cause (name-keyed type hash collisions) or at minimum adding a changelog entry and/or deprecation warning so users are aware of the behavior change.

This test exercises a different code path than the asyncio timeout mechanism. The listener raises TimeoutError synchronously (from a @ha.callback), which tests the 'except TimeoutError: pass' branch via a service-raised exception. The actual fix wraps the call in 'async with timeout(SERVICE_WAIT_TIMEOUT):', which fires only when the service takes longer than 10 seconds as an asyncio-level timeout. To test the real timeout path, use an async service handler that awaits something for longer than SERVICE_WAIT_TIMEOUT (e.g., await asyncio.sleep(20)) rather than raising TimeoutError directly.

The HTTP response is discarded. When a service raises TimeoutError, the endpoint should still return 200 OK (with the list of changed states). Without asserting on the response status and body, the test cannot verify that the API behaves correctly from the caller's perspective. Save the result and assert resp.status == 200.

The PR's primary fix — using asyncio.shield() to prevent the service call from being cancelled when the REST API client disconnects — has no test coverage. The test only covers the TimeoutError path. A test simulating client disconnection mid-call (while the service is still running) would be needed to actually verify the shield() behavior that motivated the change.

Docstring says 'does not fail on long running services' but the test service completes immediately (raises TimeoutError synchronously). The docstring is misleading; a long-running service would block asynchronously past the timeout deadline.

The comment hardcodes SP-60, but that offset is only correct when both loops and S32C1I are enabled. When loops are disabled and S32C1I is enabled, BASE_SAVE_AREA_SIZE is 48 and SCOMPARE1 lands at SP-48. The comment should say something like 'SP-60 (with loops) / SP-48 (without loops)' or simply omit the absolute offset and describe it as 'lowest word of base save area'.

The existing comment describing the intermediate SP says it 'points to low word of base save area, i.e. the saved LCOUNT or SAR'. This change adds a third possibility: with S32C1I enabled the low word is now SCOMPARE1. The comment should be updated to cover this case to avoid misleading anyone chasing a context-switch bug.

BSA_SCOMPARE1_OFF always evaluates to 0 when S32C1I is enabled (it simplifies to BASE_SAVE_AREA_SIZE_SCOMPARE - 4 = 4 - 4 = 0), but the multi-term formula obscures this and makes it look as though the offset depends on BASE_SAVE_AREA_SIZE_LOOPS. A comment or simplification (e.g. just '0') would prevent confusion. As written, a reader might incorrectly believe the offset shifts with loop configuration.

The response is never checked for success before deserializing. Unlike the original `GetFromJsonAsync`, `SendAsync` does not throw on non-2xx status codes. A 404 or 500 will silently pass a non-JSON error body to `ReadFromJsonAsync`, producing a confusing JSON parse exception instead of an HTTP error. Add `response.EnsureSuccessStatusCode();` before reading the content.

`HttpResponseMessage` implements `IDisposable` but the response is never disposed. Use `using var response = await Http.SendAsync(requestMessage);` to ensure the underlying connection resources are released promptly.

The XML doc block references a `streamingEnabled` parameter (`<param name="streamingEnabled">`) that does not exist in the method signature. The method always enables streaming (hardcodes `true`) with no way to disable it. Either add a `bool streamingEnabled` parameter and branch on it, or remove the stale `<param>` tag.

There is a stray backslash (`\`) at the end of the `</returns>` XML doc tag for `SetBrowserRequestCache` (line 47) and `SetBrowserRequestMode` (line 77). This produces malformed XML documentation. Remove the trailing backslash from both lines.

`NoCache` and `ForceCache` both have copy-pasted summaries identical to `Default` ("The browser looks for a matching request in its HTTP cache"). Their behaviors are meaningfully different: `no-cache` always revalidates with the server even if a fresh cached entry exists; `force-cache` uses a cached entry regardless of staleness. The inaccurate docs will mislead callers choosing between these modes.

The hash function uses `::towlower(ch)` (locale-sensitive) while the equality predicate was changed to `til::compare_ordinal_insensitive` (locale-insensitive/ordinal). For a hash map, equal keys must produce equal hashes — but under a locale like Turkish, `towlower(L'I')` yields `L'ı'` (not `L'i'`), so two strings that `compare_ordinal_insensitive` treats as equal can hash differently, silently breaking alias lookups. The hash function should use the same case-folding as the equality predicate (e.g., `til::tolower_ascii` or whichever function `compare_ordinal_insensitive` uses internally).

`$*` expansion uses raw pointer arithmetic: `buffer.append(args[0].data(), sourceText.data() + sourceText.size())`. This silently depends on `args[0]` being a view into `sourceText`'s contiguous storage. It is true today (args are built via `safe_slice_abs(sourceText, ...)`), but this implicit invariant is fragile — a future refactor that obtains args differently would produce undefined behaviour. Prefer computing the substring explicitly via `sourceText.substr(args[0].data() - sourceText.data())` or storing the start-of-args offset separately.

If `server.PrivateIP` is non-nil but points to an empty string (which some API responses may return for routed-IP instances), `addr` is overwritten with `""`, silently discarding a valid IPv4 or IPv6 address set in a prior block and causing the target to be skipped entirely. Guard with `if server.PrivateIP != nil && *server.PrivateIP != ""` to avoid this.

Using `:=` here introduces a new `addr` variable that shadows the outer `addr` declared at line 177. While the inner block works correctly, this is confusing and error-prone. Prefer `addr = net.JoinHostPort(...)` (plain assignment) since the outer `addr` is already declared and is not used after this block.

The phrase 'adds to table names' is inaccurate. PostgreSQL appends `_pkey` to the auto-generated *primary key index name* (derived from the table name), not to the table name itself. A user could misread this as their table name getting `_pkey` appended. Consider: '...the `_pkey` suffix PostgreSQL uses when naming the auto-generated primary key index, so our effective limit is #{table_name_length} characters.'

The base `table_name_too_long_error_message` message lacks a trailing period, while the PostgreSQL override ends with one and the nearby `validate_index_length!` error was updated to include a period. This inconsistency means non-PostgreSQL adapters produce an error sentence without a period. Add a trailing period to align with the rest.

The long PostgreSQL-specific error message string is duplicated verbatim in both this file (line 63) and `migration_test.rb` (line 211). If the message changes again, both assertions must be updated in sync. Consider extracting it to a shared helper or constant (e.g., in a test support module) to keep assertions DRY.

Each call to `installListeners()` appends a fresh SVG element with clipPath definitions to the highlight shadow DOM, but never removes the previous one. Since `installListeners()` is re-invoked on every `onGlobalListenersRemoved` event (see `pollingRecorder.ts`), duplicate `<clipPath>` nodes accumulate on every listener re-installation. Consider guarding this append (e.g. check if the SVG already exists, or remove it before re-appending).

`locator.evaluate()` wrapped in `expect(...).resolves.toBe()` executes once without retrying. If the dialog or its styles haven't fully applied when evaluated, the assertion can fail spuriously. Prefer `expect(glassPane.locator('> x-pw-dialog .accept > x-div')).toHaveCSS('clip-path', 'url("#icon-check")')`, which auto-retries until the condition is met.

Both branches of the `||` check `__init__.py` — the second call is an exact duplicate. The intent is almost certainly to check `__init__.pyi` in the second branch (matching the `StandardLibraryCustom` arm below), so stub-only packages (e.g. third-party type stubs that ship an `__init__.pyi` instead of an `__init__.py`) will never be recognised as regular packages for Extra/FirstParty/SitePackages/Editable search paths. This is a pre-existing bug, but the PR touched both lines and is a good opportunity to fix it.

`system_path_to_file(...) == Err(SystemPathError::IsADirectory)` is used as a proxy for `is_directory`. This is correct today because `SystemPathError` only has two variants, but it silently breaks if a new variant (e.g. `PermissionDenied`) is added: an inaccessible directory would return the wrong error and the check would return `false`, causing the module resolver to miss packages. Wrapping this in a small helper or at least adding a comment would make the fragility explicit.

`lock().unwrap()` will panic if the mutex is poisoned (e.g. a previous thread panicked while holding the lock). If this method can be called from multiple threads the panic will propagate. Using `lock().expect(...)` or propagating a `PoisonError` would at least give a clearer failure message; handling it explicitly would be safer.

Copy-paste bug: `nursery_all` is supposed to test `--select ALL` (as stated in the comment on line 245), but it passes `"--select", "E"` — the same args as `nursery_prefix`. This means the test never actually exercises the `RuleSelector::All` path and the behaviour of `select_all()` is left untested.

`is_nursery` determines nursery membership by joining path segments and comparing to the literal string `"RuleGroup::Nursery"`. This breaks silently if the path is written as a fully-qualified path (e.g. `crate::codes::RuleGroup::Nursery`) or if the enum variant is ever renamed. A more robust check would compare only the last segment's ident to `"Nursery"` (and optionally the penultimate to `"RuleGroup"`), or check only the final segment.

The inline comment says "we ignore them when looking at prefixes", but this is inside `select_all`, which governs `RuleSelector::All` (i.e. `--select ALL`), not prefix selectors. The comment should say something like "we ignore them when `--select ALL` is used" to avoid confusion.

UTC date is compared against `requested_date` values that were derived from local time (`dt_util.now()` at lines 85-87). For users in timezones ahead of UTC (e.g. UTC+1), right after midnight local time the UTC date still trails by 1 day, so the loop finds no match and returns `None`. This partially reintroduces the original midnight bug. Fix: use `dt_util.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d")` (local date) to match how the API request dates are computed.

`TYPE_CHECKING` is always `False` at runtime, so `assert current_day_data is not None` is dead code and never executes. If `get_data_current_day()` returns `None` (which it can), the immediately following `current_day_data.currency` on line 226 will raise `AttributeError`. The same pattern is repeated in `NordpoolSensor.native_value` (line 270), `NordpoolBlockPriceSensor.native_value` (line 324), and `NordpoolDailyAveragePriceSensor.native_value` (line 351). These need real runtime guards, e.g. `if current_day_data is None: return None` or raising/logging an error.

`dt_util.now()` is called three times independently. If a call happens right at midnight, the three invocations could return different calendar dates (e.g. yesterday/today/today or today/today/tomorrow shifted by one), producing an inconsistent set. Capture `now = dt_util.now()` once before building the list.

The cast to `uint8_t` works but is non-idiomatic and relies on `<stdint.h>` being transitively included (not a direct include of this header). The standard C idiom for this pattern is `(unsigned char) p`, which needs no header and is the conventional way to handle signed/unsigned char ambiguity in character comparisons (same technique used by `<ctype.h>` implementations). Consider: `return (unsigned char) p < ' ' || p == 127;`

Non-atomic initialization creates a race window: `_isInitialized` is set to `true` at line 63 before the delegate fields are assigned at lines 65-67. Without a memory barrier or `volatile`, a concurrent thread that calls `EnsureInitialized()` can observe `_isInitialized == true` while `_tlsConfigurationLoader` is still null, causing a NullReferenceException when the `!`-suppressed invocations on lines 79-107 are reached. Consider using a `lock` or `LazyInitializer.EnsureInitialized` pattern, or at minimum declare the delegate fields as `volatile`.

The accompanying comment explicitly states that reaching this code without HTTPS configuration initialized could cause the wrong certificate (dev cert) to silently take precedence over one configured by the user. Using `Debug.Assert` means this invariant is not enforced in release builds, so a misconfigured host would silently use the development certificate in production. This should be a proper `throw new InvalidOperationException(...)` rather than a debug-only assertion.

`AddSingleton` is used instead of `TryAddSingleton`. Because `UseKestrel()` chains `UseKestrelSlim().UseHttpsConfiguration()`, any user code that also calls `.UseHttpsConfiguration()` (or any library that calls `UseKestrel` followed by `UseHttpsConfiguration`) will register a second `HttpsConfigurationService.Initializer` singleton, quietly accumulating redundant entries in the service container. Use `TryAddSingleton` to make this registration idempotent, consistent with the rest of the Kestrel DI setup.

The previous constructor validated `options`, `configuration`, and (implicitly) the logger parameters with explicit `ArgumentNullException` throws. Those checks were dropped in this refactor. A null `configuration` will now produce a NullReferenceException inside `ConfigurationReader`'s constructor (line 34), and a null `options` or `httpsConfigurationService` will surface as an NRE at first use, both of which are harder to diagnose than early ArgumentNullExceptions. Restoring the null checks for these three parameters would maintain the original defensive contract.

`roperator.rmod` is still mapped to `"%"`, so numexpr will be used for reverse mod (e.g. `4 % series`). For large Series with negative values this produces C-style (negative) results instead of Python semantics — the same bug that was just fixed for `operator.mod`. It should also be set to `None`.

`.buffered(50)` is very aggressive for PEP 517 builds, which each spawn subprocesses and can be I/O- and CPU-heavy. In practice users rarely supply more than a handful of source trees, but even a few concurrent builds at this limit could cause resource exhaustion. Consider bounding by CPU count (e.g., `num_cpus::get()`) or making it a configurable parameter.

The error message omits the path that failed conversion, making this hard to diagnose. Include `path.display()` in the message, e.g. `anyhow::anyhow!("Failed to convert path to URL: {}", path.display())`.

When `source_trees` is non-empty, the unused-extras check is skipped entirely — even after `SourceTreeResolver` completes and the resolved metadata is available. This means `--extra nonexistent` on a `pyproject.toml` with dynamic dependencies silently succeeds with no warning. The metadata returned by `SourceTreeResolver` contains `provides_extras`; that information could be used post-resolution to validate requested extras.

The panic message for `AccessConflict::All` says "accesses component(s) in a way that conflicts" with an empty component list, which is confusing. Users won't understand why nothing is listed or that the conflict is because *all* components are accessed (e.g., via `EntityMut`). Consider a dedicated message branch for `All`, such as "accesses all components (via EntityMut or similar)".

`AccessConflict::All` is recorded unconditionally without consulting `ignored_ambiguities`, unlike the `Individual` branch which filters by it. This means per-component ambiguity suppressions (`Schedule::ignore_ambiguity`) can never silence `EntityMut`/write-all type conflicts. System-pair-level `ambiguous_with` still works (filtered above at line 1353), but the per-component global suppression mechanism is silently skipped. Consider documenting this limitation or returning early if the conflict can be attributed to no specific component and both systems are already declared ambiguous.

Replacing the hand-written `Debug` impl (which used `FormattedBitSet<T>` to render `ComponentId(N)` entries) with `#[derive(Debug)]` means `AccessConflict::Individual` now shows the raw `FixedBitSet` internal representation instead of typed component IDs. Since `AccessConflict` is public and its debug output appears in diagnostic messages, this is a minor but real regression in debuggability. If preserving this output matters, the custom impl should be kept (but made non-generic, matching the new type).

Blocking I/O inside an async function. `from_project` was changed to `async fn`, but the workspace member `pyproject.toml` files (and the workspace root at line 323) are still read with synchronous `fs_err::read_to_string`, which blocks the Tokio executor. These should use `fs_err::tokio::read_to_string(&pyproject_path).await?` — the same pattern already used in `find_workspace`.

Same blocking I/O issue as line 359: synchronous `fs_err::read_to_string` inside the newly-async `from_project`, used to re-read the workspace root `pyproject.toml`. Should be the async variant `.await?`.

Copy-paste bug: `test_albatross_root_workspace_albatross` installs from the `bird-feeder` directory (same path as `test_albatross_root_workspace_bird_feeder`) instead of the `albatross` directory.
