The snapshot output also shows `bird-feeder` being installed, confirming this test never actually exercises the albatross package.

Silent skip in the BFS traversal: if a package name is enqueued (from a workspace editable dependency) but is not present in `workspace.packages()`, the loop silently `continue`s and drops all of that package's transitive requirements. A missing workspace package at this stage likely indicates a bug (name mismatch or stale workspace state) and should produce a warning or error rather than a silent no-op.

The `Preserve` variant is left in place but the issue (#1065) explicitly states it is a misunderstanding of the `tsc` option and should be removed. Users who have explicitly configured `"preserve"` in their SWC config will continue to get semantically incorrect behavior (keeping side-effect-only import statements that tsc would drop). At minimum, this variant should be `#[deprecated]` to signal that explicit use is also wrong, not just the default.

Changing `Default` from `Preserve` to `Remove` is a silent breaking change for any caller that relies on `strip()` or `Config::default()`. The public `strip()` function now drops formerly-preserved side-effect imports with no opt-out path for existing users. The PR description acknowledges this is a breaking change and asks for a release, but there is no semver version bump or changelog entry in the diff. This should at minimum be tracked as a major version bump to avoid breaking downstream consumers silently.
`noMouseDown` is declared but never used in the test. Remove it to avoid dead code.

The comment `+(0, 21)` is misleading — the `21` is the font height in pixels, but the expectedAnchor is a terminal buffer coordinate, so adding font height makes no sense here. The correct explanation is: the viewport top is at row `historySize` (after filling the buffer), and the click was at viewport row 5, giving absolute terminal row `historySize + 5`.

Comment says "1 row lower" but `Y -= 1` moves the anchor to an earlier (higher) row in the buffer. The correct description is that the anchor shifts one row *up* in the buffer (i.e., its absolute row decrements by 1 as the circular buffer scrolls), keeping the selection visually in place. The comment contradicts the operation and will confuse future readers.

The PR description explicitly states "We also invalidate the previous selection rects", but the test never verifies that the old selection rects were invalidated after the circular-buffer increment. Consider asserting that `GetSelectionRects()` returns a fresh/correct set of rects (not stale pre-scroll ones) after `conn->WriteInput`.

Changing from `UnicodeText` to `Text` creates a schema inconsistency for MySQL. SQLAlchemy's `UnicodeText` maps to MySQL `LONGTEXT`, while `Text` maps to MySQL `TEXT` (64KB) — the very limit this PR is trying to fix. Although `airflow db init` runs migrations (which correctly apply MEDIUMTEXT), any direct `create_all()` usage (e.g. test setups, isolated tooling) will produce a TEXT column with the 64KB limit. Additionally, `alembic revision --autogenerate` will detect drift between the model (`TEXT`) and the migrated DB (`MEDIUMTEXT`) and generate a spurious migration to revert it. The model should keep `UnicodeText` or use `Text().with_variant(mysql.MEDIUMTEXT(), 'mysql')` to stay consistent with what the migration produces.

Performance regression: `get_conversion_function` is now called unconditionally on every invocation, defeating the purpose of the LUT. The comment at lines 400-402 still says this function 'is only ran if the object's type is not already in the lookup table', but that's no longer true. Previously, `or_insert_with(|| get_conversion_function(ob, py))` ensured the expensive type resolution (isinstance checks, MRO walk, NumPy scalar extraction) was skipped for cached types. The fix should move the call back inside `or_insert_with`, but that requires a separate mechanism for the `allow_object` error path (e.g., check the LUT first and only call `get_conversion_function` on cache miss, then validate `allow_object` separately).

Regression: the `else if ob.hasattr(intern!(py, "_s")).unwrap()` branch that dispatched Polars Series objects to `get_list_from_series` was removed from `get_conversion_function`. `get_list_from_series` (line 366) is still defined and used internally by `get_list`, but it is no longer reachable via the LUT for direct Series inputs. A Polars Series passed to `py_object_to_any_value` will now fall through to `get_object` (if `allow_object=true`) or raise an error, instead of being correctly converted to `AnyValue::List`. The `_s` check should be restored before the `else` branch.

NameError on the fast (cached) code path. `now` is now defined only inside the `with lock:` block (line 1152), which lives inside the `else:` branch of `if os.path.exists(cached_module_filename):` (line 1130). When the cache is warm that entire `else:` branch is skipped, so `now` is never assigned, and `now.year` / `now.month` / etc. will raise `NameError: name 'now' is not defined` for every subsequent task invocation. The fix is to move `now = datetime.datetime.utcnow()` back to its original position at the 8-space indentation level (outside both the `if` and `else` branches), so it is always executed before `output.write`.
The old assignment was commented out instead of deleted. Dead commented-out code should be removed to avoid confusion about whether it is intentional or accidental.

The comment `#moved from 1246` is an implementation note that encodes a stale line-number reference. Line numbers shift constantly, so this will be misleading within the next edit. Remove it or replace with a comment that explains *why* `now` must be initialised before the cache-existence check.

Including `errcode.ErrorCodeDenied` in `isNotFound` is semantically wrong and potentially unsafe. `ErrorCodeDenied` signals an authorization failure, not a missing resource. Triggering the tag-based fallback on a denied digest fetch can cause a TOCTOU-style issue: the tag may have been updated between the initial tag->digest resolution and the fallback fetch, causing a different (possibly untrusted) manifest to be pulled. It also shows a misleading 'non-conformant registry' warning when the real problem is authorization. Only `v2.ErrorCodeManifestUnknown` and `v2.ErrorCodeNameUnknown` should trigger the fallback.

`isNotFound` returns `true` if *any* error in an `errcode.Errors` collection is a not-found error, while `TranslatePullError` (and general registry convention) treats only the first error as significant and logs the rest. If a response contains a primary non-not-found error followed by a secondary not-found error, `isNotFound` will incorrectly trigger the tag fallback. To be consistent, it should check only the first error in the collection.

`errors.Wrap(err, ...)` is called unconditionally after `ms.Get`, including when `ms.Get` succeeds (err == nil). `pkg/errors.Wrap(nil, msg)` returns nil so there is no functional bug, but wrapping unconditionally buries the intent: the message 'error after falling back to get manifest by tag' is only meaningful on failure. Moving this into an explicit `if err != nil { err = errors.Wrap(...) }` block makes the success path clearer and avoids relying on the nil-wrap no-op.

The fixed 512-wchar (1024-byte) buffer is too small for arbitrary REG_MULTI_SZ policy values. If the registry data exceeds this size, RegGetValueW returns ERROR_MORE_DATA (non-zero), the condition fails silently, and the loop falls through to HKCU or skips the policy entirely. For a security policy feature this silent bypass is a correctness concern. The standard fix is to call RegGetValueW once with a null buffer to query the required size, allocate accordingly, then call again.

The loop iterates HKLM then HKCU and breaks on first success, which is correct for policy precedence. However, user JSON DisabledProfileSources (lines 146-153) is always applied before the registry loop, making the final set a union of both. An HKLM policy that sets an empty list cannot override or clear a user's JSON list — it simply finds no registry value (or an empty MULTI_SZ) and does not break, then HKCU is checked. This may be intentional, but if policy is meant to be the authoritative source, the user JSON sources should be cleared before applying the winning registry policy key.

Misleading function name inside a `#[should_panic]` test: `compatible_filter` is named as if the parameters are compatible, but the test expects a panic because `&mut Handle<MyAsset>` conflicts with `AssetChanged<MyAsset>`. Rename to `incompatible_filter` to match the test's intent.

Incorrect comment: "First run of the app, `add_systems(Startup…)` runs." The `added` test does not register any Startup system, so this comment is wrong. It was likely copy-pasted from the `changed` test where a Startup system is used.

`Added` and `LoadedWithDependencies` events are treated identically to `Modified`, causing `AssetChanged<A>` to fire for freshly added or dependency-loaded assets, not just for mutations of existing assets. This behaviour is intentional (there's an `added` test for it) but the primary `AssetChanged` doc comment only mentions "changed" assets. The doc should explicitly state that `Added` and `LoadedWithDependencies` events are also tracked, so users aren't surprised.

`AssetOrHandleChanged<A>` is exported in the prelude but has no dedicated test. The individual parts (`Changed<Handle<A>>` and `AssetChanged<A>`) are tested, but the combined `Or<...>` alias is not exercised to confirm it correctly fires when only the handle changes, only the asset changes, or both change in the same frame.

Potential NullPointerException: `map.get(0)` can return null if queue 0 was never created for this LMQ topic, yet `cq.destroy()` and the subsequent `cq.getTopic()`/`cq.getQueueId()` log calls are made unconditionally. Add a null check for `cq` before using it.

The `checkLmqMessage` check fires for any message that has `PROPERTY_INNER_MULTI_DISPATCH` set, even if all targeted LMQ topics already exist and no new queues would be created. This means legitimate messages to pre-existing LMQ topics are rejected at capacity, which is more restrictive than the intent (limiting the *number* of distinct LMQ topics). The check should be skipped (or differentiated) when the target LMQ topics already exist in the consume queue table.

`getConsumeQueue` returning null when the LMQ limit is exceeded (rather than only during `asyncPutMessage`) may cause NullPointerExceptions in callers that use this method for reads, offset queries, or stats — not just writes. The limit guard should apply only on the write path, not on general consume queue lookups.

`lmqConsumeQueueNum.getAndDecrement()` is called even when `cq` is null (since it is inside the `if (map != null)` block but `map.get(0)` is not guarded). If the NPE on line 3318 is caught or if an early return is added for the null-cq case, the decrement must still happen; otherwise the counter drifts negative. Tie the decrement to a successful queue removal rather than the map-existence check.

REPLY_POLICY should be ONE_SUCCEEDED, not ALL_SUCCEEDED. The PR description explicitly states: "ONE_SUCCEEDED - ... Used by SCRIPT LOAD. This is meant to return the script's SHA1 digest, which can be done based on any of the shards' replies." Using ALL_SUCCEEDED would fail if any shard returns an error, defeating the purpose of loading to all shards.

The "random" flag is silently ignored with no warning or log message. Module authors using RM_CreateCommand with "random" will have no indication their flag is now a no-op, which could cause unexpected behavior in modules that rely on this flag for script determinism. Consider emitting a server log warning (e.g. serverLog(LL_WARNING, ...)) so module maintainers can migrate.

The new tips table name uses lowercase `_tips` (e.g. `SET_tips`) while the history table name uses PascalCase `_History` (e.g. `SET_History`), and the old hints used `_Hints`. This naming inconsistency is baked into all generated C identifiers in commands.c. Consider `_Tips` for consistency with `_History`.

Removing the fallback `elif resume_from_checkpoint is None: resume_from_checkpoint = args.resume_from_checkpoint` is a silent breaking change for any user who calls `trainer.train()` directly (without an explicit argument) while relying on `TrainingArguments.resume_from_checkpoint` being honoured automatically. Their checkpoint will now be silently ignored — no warning, no error. At minimum, a deprecation warning should be emitted if `args.resume_from_checkpoint` is set but no explicit argument was passed to `train()`, so callers know they must now forward the value themselves.

The old code guarded against using a local `model_name_or_path` as a checkpoint when `num_labels` didn't match. That guard is intentionally removed here, which is fine. However, the new path — taking `training_args.resume_from_checkpoint` verbatim — has no existence check before passing it to `trainer.train()`. The Trainer itself only validates that `WEIGHTS_NAME` exists inside the folder, so a typo in the path will raise an opaque `ValueError` deep inside training. A simple `os.path.isdir(checkpoint)` guard (consistent with the `last_checkpoint` path already established) would give a clearer error earlier, and would be consistent with how the `output_dir` detection already works.
Debug statement accidentally left in production code. `_LOGGER.error(user_input)` logs the raw user selection at ERROR level on every agreement-selection form submission. Remove this line.

Copy-paste bug: `power_meter_reading_low` ("Electricity Meter Feed IN Tariff 2") sets `ATTR_MEASUREMENT` to `"meter_high"`, the same value as `power_meter_reading` (Tariff 1). It should read `"meter_low"` (or the correct low-tariff field name). Both sensors will otherwise report identical values.

`unregister_webhook` unconditionally reads `self.entry.data[CONF_WEBHOOK_ID]`, but webhook registration is deferred (runs asynchronously after HA starts). If the entry is unloaded before `register_webhook` has run, `CONF_WEBHOOK_ID` won't be in `entry.data` and this will raise a `KeyError`. Guard with `if CONF_WEBHOOK_ID not in self.entry.data: return` at the top of the method.

`register_webhook` registers a new `EVENT_HOMEASSISTANT_STOP` listener every time it is called, including on webhook-expiry re-registration (code 510). Over time this accumulates duplicate listeners and causes `unregister_webhook` to be called multiple times on shutdown. Store and cancel any previously registered listener before adding a new one, or use a flag/guard to prevent re-adding.

`activate_agreement` is called without any error handling. If it raises a `ToonError` (e.g., network failure or invalid agreement), the exception propagates uncaught instead of being converted to `ConfigEntryNotReady`, which would cause HA to retry setup automatically. Wrap this call in a try/except that raises `ConfigEntryNotReady`.

When `find_next_selection_after_deletion` returns `None` (deleted item was the last visible entry), the fallback calls `select_last`, which picks `worktree.entries(true, 0).last()` — the alphabetically-last path in the worktree. This does not match the visual predecessor. In a tree that mixes dirs and files (dirs rendered first), the visual predecessor of the last root-level file can be a file in the same directory, while the alphabetically-last full path may be a deeply-nested item. The PR description says 'Focus on prev file/dir in case where it's last item in worktree', but the implementation doesn't achieve this. The fix would be to search `visible_entries` backwards from the last-deleted entry, skipping other marked entries, instead of calling `select_last`.

The refactored test suite dropped the only test that verified 'Focus on prev file/dir in case where it's last item in worktree' (explicitly stated in the PR description). The original `test_next_selection_after_delete_file` had a case where `third.rs` — the last visible item — was deleted and `first.rs` (its predecessor) was selected. None of the new tests exercise this path: every deletion scenario always has a next item available. Adding a test case that deletes `file6.txt` (visually last) and verifies the visual predecessor is selected would cover the fallback `select_last` code path and expose whether it behaves correctly.

`next_entry` is found by scanning the pre-deletion `visible_entries` starting right after `last_marked_entry_without_marked_ancestors`. When the very first candidate entry is itself marked for deletion (e.g., two non-contiguous marked files where the second is adjacent to a third marked file), the resolved `next_entry` can be an item that will also be deleted, causing the selection to land on a deleted entry after the operation completes. The scan should skip entries whose `SelectedEntry` is in `self.marked_entries`.

Broadcasting the same PAC records (built with conn=NULL) to all connected clients via bt_gatt_notify_uuid(NULL, ...) is incorrect if publish_capability performs per-connection filtering. A single NULL-conn query produces one capability set, but different clients may legitimately receive different capabilities. The correct fix is to iterate over all active connections and call get_pac_records + bt_gatt_notify_uuid once per connection.

The new `type` variable (like the pre-existing `uuid`) is left uninitialized if neither conditional branch executes — e.g., when both CONFIG_BT_PAC_SNK and CONFIG_BT_PAC_SRC are defined but work matches neither (a programming error elsewhere). Passing uninitialized `type` to get_pac_records is undefined behaviour. Using `else if` for the second branch, or asserting after both blocks that uuid/type are set, would catch this.

The `Time -> Datetime` error guard was moved from `SeriesTrait::cast` in `dates_time.rs` to `LogicalType::cast` in `time.rs`, but no test covers this behavior after the refactor. If the call chain from `dates_time.rs`'s `_ => self.0.cast(data_type)` doesn't route through `LogicalType::cast` for `TimeChunked` as expected, this guard would silently stop working. Adding a test like `pytest.raises(pl.ComputeError, match="cannot cast.*Time.*Datetime")` for `pl.Series([time(0)]).cast(pl.Datetime)` would prevent a regression going undetected.

The `Time -> Datetime` error was moved here from `SeriesTrait::cast` in `dates_time.rs`. The correctness of this relies on `self.0.cast(dtype)` in `dates_time.rs`'s macro-generated `SeriesTrait::cast` routing through `LogicalType::cast` for `TimeChunked`. If `ChunkedArray<TimeType>` resolves `.cast()` to an inherent method rather than `LogicalType::cast`, this arm is dead code and the restriction silently disappears. The `Duration` arm was already there and presumably works, but it's worth verifying the `Time -> Datetime` path is actually exercised.

The `is_numpy_dev` guard only handles numpy nightly builds. If numpy stable adopts this restriction (rejecting complex arrays in percentile/quantile), the test will start failing again as `is_numpy_dev` will be False for the stable release. Consider also guarding on a minimum numpy version (e.g., `np_version_under1p25` or similar) once the exact stable version that introduces this change is known.

The root cause of the CI failure is that `series.quantile()` (which calls numpy percentile) now rejects complex arrays in numpy dev. The test change only adapts the test to expect the error; the underlying `describe()` call is still broken for complex-typed Series with new numpy. The fix should be in `describe_numeric_1d`: either raise a clear pandas-level error for complex inputs before hitting numpy, or compute percentiles on the real/imaginary parts separately. Without a fix here, users with numpy nightly will get an unhelpful `TypeError` from numpy internals.

The `match` string is tightly coupled to a numpy-internal error message. If numpy changes its wording, the `pytest.raises` block will pass but for the wrong reason (a different exception). Consider using a less exact match or catching on a specific numpy version boundary instead.

In `derive_filter_fetch_impl`, the generated struct stores `<field_types as WorldQuery>::Fetch` fields (line 300), but `init` initializes them with `ReadOnlyFetch::init`. This is a type mismatch for any type where `Fetch != ReadOnlyFetch`, producing a confusing compile error. The struct fields should either be changed to `ReadOnlyFetch` (making it consistent with how `init` works), or `init` should use `Fetch::init` instead.

`ReadOnlyFetch` is unconditionally implemented for the generated filter struct without any static assertion verifying that the stored `Fetch` instances are actually read-only. `derive_fetch_impl` generates `assert_readonly` checks for `read_only`-annotated structs; an equivalent check should guard this impl to prevent unsound code if a user accidentally includes a non-read-only type in a filter struct.

Copy-paste error in the panic message: `.expect("Mut is expected to have a lifetime")` is used when extracting the *type* argument (not the lifetime) from `Mut<'_, T>` inside `Option<Mut<T>>` handling. The same mistake recurs at line 654 in the standalone `Mut` branch. The message should say something like "Mut is expected to have a type argument" to make debugging less confusing.

Two stray doc lines appear outside any code fence: `/// # use bevy_ecs::prelude::*;` and `/// use bevy_ecs::query::{Fetch, ReadOnlyFetch, WorldQuery};`. In rustdoc, the `# ` prefix creates an h1 heading, so the first line will render as a section heading with the text `use bevy_ecs::prelude::*;`. These look like leftover lines from a draft that should be removed — the correct versions already appear inside the code block that follows.

`CLIENT_STATIC_ATTRIBUTES + CLIENT_CONNECTED_ATTRIBUTES` creates a new list object on every call to `device_state_attributes`. Since this property is polled frequently in Home Assistant (on each state update, for every tracked device), the allocation is wasteful. Define a single combined constant at module level, e.g. `CLIENT_ALL_ATTRIBUTES = CLIENT_STATIC_ATTRIBUTES + CLIENT_CONNECTED_ATTRIBUTES`.

RGB565 green channel is shifted by 6 bits instead of the correct 5 bits. In RGB565 the 6-bit green field occupies bits [10:5], so it must be `g << 5`. Using `g << 6` displaces the green component one bit high, corrupting the image colours.

When the I2C read succeeds (ret == 0) but the sensor ID is wrong, the function returns `ret` which equals 0 (success) instead of an error. The caller then proceeds with a misidentified device. Fix: return `-ENODEV` (or similar) when the ID does not match, regardless of `ret`.

Self-referential initialisation `vbuf = vbuf` is undefined behaviour in C (reading an uninitialized variable to initialise itself). Compilers may use this pattern to suppress warnings, but it is not safe. Initialise to `NULL` instead.

The fake register bank is a non-static global. When `DT_INST_FOREACH_STATUS_OKAY` instantiates more than one device, all instances share the same array, causing them to read each other's register state. It should be moved into `struct emul_imager_data` (or at minimum marked `static`).

The mode tables (`emul_imager_rgb565_64x20_modes`, `emul_imager_yuyv_64x20_modes`) are neither `static` nor `const`. Missing `static` gives them external linkage, risking symbol collisions in multi-file builds or when this skeleton is copied. Missing `const` allows accidental mutation of configuration data. Both qualifiers should be added.

The worker copies directly from `cfg->source_dev->data` relying on the framebuffer being the first field of `struct emul_imager_data`. This is fragile: any reordering of that struct silently breaks the copy with no compile-time error. A better approach is to expose an accessor function or a typed pointer in the source driver's API rather than casting an opaque `void *`.

The ValueError fallback path (triggered when GroupByApply.agg() raises 'No objects to concatenate') calls _aggregate_frame(func) without forwarding args/kwargs. With the new routing logic, multi-column DataFrames with kwargs now reach the GroupByApply branch, so if agg() fails here, the fallback silently drops the caller's kwargs and may produce wrong results. Consider passing args/kwargs to the fallback: _aggregate_frame(func, *args, **kwargs).

The test only exercises the kwargs path on a multi-column DataFrame. Two important cases are missing: (1) a single-column DataFrame with kwargs, which still routes to _aggregate_frame (different code path, regression risk), and (2) positional args (not kwargs), since the fix also changes args=() to args=args in the GroupByApply call and that path is untested.

The column-count guard (== 1) is the only thing separating _aggregate_frame from GroupByApply for UDFs with args/kwargs. If _obj_with_exclusions ever has columns excluded (e.g. via exclude= on groupby), the count could be 1 even when the user is operating on a logically multi-column object, routing to _aggregate_frame unexpectedly. A comment explaining the invariant here would reduce future breakage risk.

`_focusableElements` is populated in the constructor (lines 22-26) but `ContainsFocus()` — the only method that read it — was deleted. This set is now dead code that allocates memory and inserts 5 elements on every SearchBoxControl instantiation for no purpose. Remove the inserts in the constructor and the `_focusableElements` member declaration in the header.

`OnGotFocus` is declared without the `override` keyword. In C++/WinRT, if the signature doesn't exactly match the base class virtual, the compiler silently treats it as a new (unrelated) method and the XAML framework never invokes it via virtual dispatch. Focus funneling into the TextBox would silently do nothing. Add `override` here (and the same in `TermControl.h` line 60) to catch any mismatch at compile time.

The previous `SetFocusOnTextbox()` guarded with `if (TextBox())` before use. `OnGotFocus` drops that guard. If this handler fires before the control template is fully applied (e.g., in certain deferred-load scenarios), `TextBox()` could be null and the call will crash. Restore the null check: `if (auto tb = TextBox()) { tb.Focus(...); tb.SelectAll(); }`.

Same missing `override` issue as in `SearchBoxControl.h`: `OnGotFocus` is not marked `override`, so a signature mismatch would compile cleanly but the method would never be called by XAML's virtual focus dispatch, silently breaking the focus-funneling logic from TermControl to TerminalInputTarget.

Removing the `typeof textContent === 'string'` guard changes behavior for Document nodes (nodeType 9): `document.textContent` returns `null` per spec, so `return elem.textContent` now returns `null` where the old code fell into the `else` branch and traversed children. This is a silent regression if anyone calls `jQuery(document).text()` or internally passes a Document node to `jQuery.text()`. The nodeType 9 case in the condition explicitly signals Document nodes were meant to be handled. Consider returning `elem.textContent || ''` or keeping the typeof guard.

The default `isAttached` function uses `composed` in its body, but `composed` is assigned on the next line (same `var` statement). This is technically correct—`composed` is fully assigned before `isAttached` is ever invoked—but the forward reference is easy to misread and could cause confusion during future edits. Consider reordering the declarations so `composed` is defined before `isAttached`.

The file now skips runtime feature detection entirely and unconditionally enables the `:enabled/:disabled` and `[name='']` workarounds for all IE versions. The old code proved the `[name='']` bug at runtime (only IE 11 was documented to have it, not older IEs). While the impact is harmless (a slightly more conservative QSA bypass), switching from detection to assumption means any future IE-variant environment that doesn't have these bugs also gets the workarounds silently applied.

The `while` loop is correct for draining tasks added during setup, but it can loop indefinitely: since `_setup_complete` remains `False` throughout, any callback triggered during `await asyncio.gather(*pending)` (line 207) that calls `_async_schedule_add_entities` will append a new task to `self._tasks`, causing another iteration. In a pathological case—e.g., a platform whose entity `async_update` or a listener schedules additional entity batches—`_async_setup_platform` would never complete and `_setup_complete` would never be set. The old `if` was wrong (left stale references), but the `while` trades correctness for a potential liveness hazard. Consider capping the number of drain iterations or asserting no new tasks are added mid-loop, and document the assumption that entity-addition tasks won't recursively schedule more batches during setup.

After the fix, `main_input_name` is assigned directly from `self.model.encoder.main_input_name` with no fallback. If that attribute is `None` (unusual but not impossible for a custom model), the `KeyError: None` at the batch-indexing site would still occur. Consider using `self.model.encoder.main_input_name or getattr(self.model, "main_input_name", "input_ids")` to be safe.

The comment is now inaccurate after the migration. `linkmode.IsStatic` is NOT Linux-only — it is defined with cross-platform build tags (`static_build` / `!static_build`). The reason `ensureHomeIfStatic` lives in `gcplogging_linux.go` is that the segfault it guards against (glibc / os/user.Current) is Linux-specific. The comment should say something like: "Note: this workaround is only needed on linux (the segfault is glibc-specific), so the implementation lives here rather than in gcplogging.go."

Dictionary is modified inside a `foreach` over it without a `break`, which will throw `InvalidOperationException` at runtime. Replace with a two-step approach: find the key first, then remove it outside the loop (e.g., `var key = actions.Keys.FirstOrDefault(k => ...); if (key != null) actions.Remove(key);`).

The new `Parse` implementation looks up keys by their *localized* display strings (`LocalizedKeys`, `LocalizedModifiers`), but the `ActionCommand.HotKeys` setter persists bindings using the *invariant* `HotKeyCollection.Code` (which serialises enum names like `"Ctrl+A"`). These two formats are incompatible: settings saved by the old path will silently parse to `HotKey.None` on the new path, and any persisted binding will break if the user's display language changes. Revert to `Enum.TryParse` for deserialization so the stored format remains locale-invariant.

The condition is inverted: the custom entry is added to `actions` only when the new collection *equals* the default, and is silently dropped when the user actually customises the binding. The result is that any real remapping is discarded on app restart. The condition should be negated: `if (!newCollection.Select(x => x.Label).SequenceEqual(defaultCollection.Select(x => x.Label)))`.

Uses the localized `customizedCollection.Label` (e.g., `"Strg+A"` in German) to persist the custom binding instead of the locale-invariant `customizedCollection.Code` (e.g., `"Ctrl+A"`). This makes the stored value locale-dependent; change to `customizedCollection.Code`.

`DefaultHotKeys` is initialised from `BaseCommand.HotKeys` rather than `BaseCommand.DefaultHotKeys`. If the `ActionCommand` has already had its hotkeys overridden by `UpdateHotKeys` before `ModifiableCommand` is constructed, `DefaultHotKeys` will capture the customised collection instead of the true defaults, breaking the "reset to default" check in the Actions page. Change to `BaseCommand.DefaultHotKeys`.

The `$()` command substitution wraps the entire command, so stdout is redirected to the file inside the subshell but the outer shell executes the (empty) captured output as a command. This always exits 0, meaning `rc` is never set on failure — the error check on line 21 is dead. Fix: `"$UDEV_DMI_MEMORY_ID" -F "$i" > "$D/out.txt" 2>&1 && rc= || rc=$?`

`%.1g` formats to 1 significant digit, so 1.2 V (code=1200) prints as "1" instead of "1.2". The test fixtures confirm this (CONFIGURED_VOLTAGE=1). Use `%.1f` to get one decimal place, or `%g` consistently to let libc trim trailing zeros.

`dmi_string` traverses the string table with no upper-bound on how far `bp` may advance. On malformed or adversarially crafted DMI data the loop can read past the allocated buffer. The function should receive (or compute from the outer buffer) the end of the string region and add a bounds check to the loop.

`IMPORT{program}="dmi_memory_id"` fires for every event in the `dmi` subsystem (BIOS, baseboard, chassis, CPU, etc.), not just memory-array entries. This causes redundant program executions and sets memory properties on unrelated DMI devices. Add an `ATTR{...}` or `ENV{DMI_TYPE}` match to restrict the rule to the physical memory array entry (DMI type 16).

`sizeof(int64_t) < 8` is a compile-time constant that is always false (int64_t is exactly 8 bytes by definition), so the 64-bit address guard is never enforced. The original dmidecode check was `sizeof(off_t) < 8`; consider whether that or a different runtime check was intended here.

When `typeLoadException.LoaderExceptions` is null, the code skips logging entirely and silently returns the partial type list. The original code always logged a warning for any `ReflectionTypeLoadException`. This is a regression: a `ReflectionTypeLoadException` with a null `LoaderExceptions` array (technically valid) will now be swallowed without any trace in the log. Move the `LoaderExceptions != null` guard to only gate the BadImageFormat-specific suppression logic, not all logging.

`typeLoadException.LoaderExceptions.Count()` re-enumerates the array a second time (already enumerated on line 572 for the filtered count). Since `LoaderExceptions` is `Exception[]`, prefer `.Length` for the total count: `typeLoadException.LoaderExceptions.Length != badImageFormatExceptionCount`. This also makes the intent clearer and avoids a potential confusion if null entries exist in the array (null entries count in `.Count()` but not in the predicate-filtered count on line 572, which could trigger a spurious warning when all real exceptions are `BadImageFormatException`).

No tests cover the None-data path this fix introduces. The PR checklist explicitly marks tests as not added. At minimum, a unit test should verify that get_native_value returns None when getattr returns None for a sensor key, and that the event subscription remains active afterward (since an uncaught exception in native_value was likely the root cause of subscriptions silently dying).

The autocorrect always produces single-quoted strings, but the original may use double quotes (e.g. `require "foo.rb"`). This silently changes the quote style, which can conflict with `Style/StringLiterals`. Instead, derive the quote character from `node.source` and preserve it: e.g. `quote = node.source[0]; corrector.replace(node.source_range, "#{quote}#{correction}#{quote}")`.

All test cases use single-quoted strings. There is no test for double-quoted require paths (e.g. `require "foo.rb"`), so the quote-mangling bug in autocorrect goes undetected. Add a test case covering double-quoted strings to verify the corrected output preserves the original quote style.

`buflen` is declared `unsigned`, so `buflen < 0` is always false and provides no guard. This dead check was not cleaned up when the function signature was changed to add `trunc`. Either remove it or change `buflen` to `int` if negative sentinel values are ever intended.

`wscnts` is `unsigned[]`, so `*wscnt >= 0` is always true and adds no bound check. Loop termination relies on `0xFFFF * 2` being larger than any `*llen` value (currently max ~1087). If `linelengths` ever includes a value above 32767, `0xFFFF` would no longer terminate iteration and the loop would run past the end of the `wscnts` array (UB). Consider replacing the tautological check with an explicit array-bounds sentinel (e.g., terminate `wscnts` with a 0 and guard iteration separately, or use `wscnt < wscnts + OSSL_NELEM(wscnts)`).

`BIO_new(BIO_f_base64())` can return NULL on allocation failure, but the result is used immediately without a NULL check. `BIO_set_flags(b64, ...)` and `BIO_push(b64, bio)` would dereference NULL if this fails, crashing the test runner instead of reporting a clean failure. Add a NULL check and return an appropriate error, consistent with the other BIO allocation checks in the same function.

Asserting that an explicitly `[FromBody]`-annotated parameter produces zero `ParameterDescriptions` is almost certainly wrong. An explicit `[FromBody]` binding source must appear in the API description for OpenAPI tooling (Swashbuckle, NSwag) to generate a request body schema. If the intent is to test that inferred-body params are excluded, only the first case (line 328) should assert empty; the `[FromBody]` case should still assert a body parameter is present.

The `expectedType` parameter of `AssertBodyParameter` is now dead code — it is never used in the function body. This is misleading to future readers who expect the type to be validated. Either remove the parameter or restore the type assertions.

The lambda `([FromRoute] int foo, int bar, InferredJsonClass fromBody)` has three parameters, but the test now expects only 2 in `ParameterDescriptions`. If the body parameter (`fromBody`) is intentionally excluded from descriptions in the new design, the test should document this reasoning. As written, the removed assertions and the lack of a comment make it unclear whether this is a deliberate behavioral change or an oversight.

The deleted `TestIsRequiredFromBody` test was the only test verifying that nullable body parameters (`InferredJsonClass?`) correctly set `IsRequired = false` in `ParameterDescriptions`. This is exactly the existing behavior that the issue and PR are building on; removing it leaves the core `IsRequired` behavior for inferred body parameters untested.

`('import_tasks')` is a bare string in parentheses, not a tuple. Python's `in` operator on a string performs a substring check, so any action whose name is a substring of 'import_tasks' (e.g. an action named 'tasks' or 'import') would incorrectly trigger this validation block. Use `== 'import_tasks'` or add a trailing comma: `('import_tasks',)`.

`key in ds` is always True here because `diff` is built from `set(ds.keys())`, so every key in `diff` is already in `ds`. The redundant guard is inconsistent with the analogous check at line 104 (`if ds[k] is not Sentinel`) and adds noise. Drop the `key in ds and` clause.

There are no new tests in the diff covering the added validation path. The bug report shows a concrete reproduction case (notify on import_tasks); a unit or integration test asserting an AnsibleParserError is raised (and a warning emitted when INVALID_TASK_ATTRIBUTE_FAILED is False) would prevent regressions.

Log level mismatch: `log_warning_errno` is used here but the function still returns the error, causing manager initialization to fail. Throughout this file, non-recoverable initialization failures consistently use `log_error_errno`. A WARNING implies the code continues despite the issue, but here it aborts. Use `log_error_errno` to match severity and the established pattern.

The declaration of `bt_conn_iso_init()` is not wrapped in `#if defined(CONFIG_BT_ISO)`, while its definition in `conn.c` is. The call site in `hci_core.c` uses `IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_BT_ISO)` (a runtime-style branch) rather than a preprocessor `#if`, so when CONFIG_BT_ISO=n the function is declared but never defined. GCC/Clang will typically dead-code-eliminate an `if (0)` branch before linking, but this is not guaranteed across all toolchains or optimization levels. For consistency and safety, the declaration should be guarded with `#if defined(CONFIG_BT_ISO)`, matching the definition.

visit_mut_arrow_expr copies self.ctx without updating fn_scope to the arrow's span ctxt. As a result, variables declared inside an arrow function get their in_fn_scope set to the enclosing regular function's scope (or top-level) instead of the arrow's own scope. The outer-scope guard added in sequences.rs then operates on stale scope data for arrow function bodies, which can cause the guard to fire when it shouldn't (blocking valid optimizations) or to miss cross-scope cases entirely. Fix: update fn_scope analogously to how visit_mut_function does it, e.g. create a new Ctx with fn_scope: n.span.ctxt before calling visit_mut_children_with.

usage.ref_count is incremented here, but ref_count tracks read references (see its uses in ref_count != 1 / ref_count == 1 guards throughout iife.rs for inlining decisions). Converting var x = init to an assignment x = init is a WRITE, so assign_count should be incremented instead. Over-counting reads causes downstream single-use inlining checks (e.g. ref_count != 1 at line 187) to incorrectly skip remapped variables.

The Mergable::FnDecl arm is missing the outer-scope guard that was added to the Var and Expr arms. A named function expression or hoisted function declaration that closes over outer mutable variables (e.g. depth) could still be incorrectly sequenced. The existing TODO acknowledges uncertainty; this check should at least be considered here for consistency with the fix applied to the other arms.

v.in_fn_scope is unconditionally overwritten on every call to on_declare_decl. If the same variable is declared in multiple passes or in multiple hoisting positions (var declarations hoisted to function scope can appear at both the declaration site and the usage site during analysis), the last-processed call wins. This is especially fragile given the fix relies on in_fn_scope being stable across the analysis and optimization phases.

Spurious non-null assertion on the boolean literal `true`. `true!` is valid TypeScript but is a clear typo—compare with the identical call for the non-iframe branch which correctly passes `true`. No runtime impact (the `!` is erased at compile time), but it's confusing and should be `true` without the `!`.

Double-wrapping: `elementHandle.setInputFiles` already calls `_wrapApiCall('elementHandle.setInputFiles', ...)` internally (elementHandle.ts:182). Wrapping it again here with `_wrapApiCall('fileChooser.setFiles', ...)` will produce two separate API log entries for a single user action. Consider delegating without the extra wrapper, or suppressing the inner one.

`trimUrl` silently returns `undefined` for RegExp predicates, so `waitForRequest`/`waitForResponse` produce no URL log line when a RegExp is passed. The originating issue asks for logging 'the URL or the predicate'. Consider also handling the RegExp case, e.g. returning `urlOrPredicate.toString()` (possibly truncated), so all call shapes are covered.

The test uses non-overlapping `playerID` values across the two DataFrames, so the inner join always produces an empty result. The schema check is sufficient to catch the reported `ColumnNotFoundError`, but a test variant with at least one matching join key would also validate that the optimized plan correctly propagates actual rows through the join — providing stronger confidence that the fix is correct end-to-end.

When `obj` IS a namespace identifier but `prop` is a non-string computed expression (e.g. `CONSTANTS[someVar]`), `return` exits `visit_mut_expr` entirely without visiting children. Any const-module references inside `someVar` will silently go untransformed. The PR adds the missing `else` branch for the ident-not-in-namespace case, but the symmetric case—ident IS in namespace, prop is unresolvable—still bails out without calling `n.visit_mut_children_with(self)`. Consider replacing `return` here (and at line 175) with `{ n.visit_mut_children_with(self); return; }` so child expressions are still processed.

Redundant ternary: `embeddedSchema.prefix !== undefined ? embeddedSchema.prefix : undefined` always evaluates to `embeddedSchema.prefix`. This can be simplified to just `prefix: embeddedSchema.prefix`. While not a bug, it may mislead future readers into thinking there is intentional conditional logic here.

A new `EntitySchemaTransformer` is instantiated and `transform()` is called on every invocation of `createEmbeddedsRecursively` for each EntitySchema-based embedded. This means the same schema is re-transformed every time an entity's metadata is built, and again for every level of nesting. For a deeply-nested or widely-shared embedded schema this can be expensive. Consider caching the transformed `MetadataArgsStorage` keyed by the EntitySchema instance (e.g. via a `WeakMap`) or computing it once outside the recursive call.

When `type` is an `EntitySchema`, `create()` returns a bare `{}`. For decorator-based embeds, `new (this.type as any)` runs the constructor which may set default property values. A bare `{}` is fine if TypeORM always fully hydrates every embedded from the database row, but if an embedded object is used before hydration (e.g. in `save()` on a partial entity), nested embedded properties will be missing rather than initialised to their defaults. Confirm that all code paths that call `create()` subsequently write every column value before the object is consumed.

The test was changed from expecting a ViewCreationFailedException to expecting success, which exercises the new fallback path (tool visible from target but not from definition). However, the test name `testToolVisibilityUseCheckAtRule` previously meant 'definition-based check fails when only use-site has visibility' — the opposite of what it now asserts. This inverted meaning will mislead readers trying to understand which test covers the failure path vs. the fallback-success path. Consider renaming to reflect the fallback behavior, e.g., `testToolVisibilityUseFallbackSucceedsCheckAtRule`.

The comment at line 142 says the error message should 'always suggest making the prerequisite visible from the definition, not the target,' but no test verifies the error message content. `testToolVisibilityPrivateCheckAtRule` only checks that an exception is thrown, not that `handleVisibilityConflict` is called with `implicitDefinition` (rather than `rule.getLabel()`). If the argument were accidentally swapped or the method used the wrong label for its suggestion, it would go undetected.

The fallback `context.isVisible(prerequisite.getConfiguredTarget())` re-runs the old target-based check whenever the new definition-based check fails. This means every implicit-attribute visibility failure now incurs two visibility checks instead of one. For large builds with many Starlark rules and implicit attributes this could add measurable overhead. Consider short-circuiting or caching the result of the first check if both are expensive.

In the error catch block, `resolveFrom` is used to read the config file for the diagnostic message. However, earlier in the function (around line 37), `resolveFrom` may have been reassigned to `path.join(options.projectRoot, 'index')` — a synthetic, non-existent path. Reading this path in the catch block will throw a secondary file-not-found error and lose the original useful diagnostic. The original config path should be preserved in a separate variable before reassignment, e.g. `let originalResolveFrom = resolveFrom;` and use that in the catch.

The previous code wrapped `preprocessor.render(content, {})` in a try/catch that specifically handled `MODULE_NOT_FOUND` to auto-install the missing template preprocessor (e.g. `pug`, `sass`). Vue templates can specify arbitrary `lang="..."` attributes whose preprocessors are only known at transform time, not at install time, so they cannot be listed in `parcelDependencies` ahead of use. Removing this handling means users will now get a raw, unhelpful `MODULE_NOT_FOUND` error when using `<template lang="pug">` or similar, with no recovery path. The removal silently regresses a user-facing feature; at minimum, catch and re-throw with an actionable message pointing to the missing package.

`resolveFrom` is silently mutated from its original value (the config file path inside `node_modules`) to a project-root synthetic path. This mutation is the root cause of the error-path bug (line 107) but also makes the function harder to reason about. Consider using a separate variable (e.g. `effectiveResolveFrom`) for the resolution call while keeping `resolveFrom` pointing to the actual config file for diagnostics.

The original `get_print_mode().unwrap_or_else(|| args.mode())` in `FitsMeasurer` was intentionally defensive: it handled forward references where a conditional element (ConditionalGroup, ConditionalContent, IndentIfGroupBreaks, FitsExpanded) references a group ID that appears later in the document and hasn't been registered yet. Replacing it with `unwrap_print_mode()` (which panics) is only safe if ALL such group IDs are guaranteed to be pre-registered before any `FitsMeasurer` traversal encounters them. The new pre-registration in `print_group` covers one code path (the flat measurement branch), but there are other measurement scenarios—such as a `best_fitting` outer measurement whose variants contain forward-referencing conditional elements—where the group ID may not yet be in `group_modes`, causing a formatter panic. Consider keeping `unwrap_or_else(|| args.mode())` as the fallback in `FitsMeasurer`, or add a broader pre-registration step.

The magic threshold `10` is unexplained: identifiers of 10 bytes or fewer will never be parenthesized, even if they cause line overflow. Additionally, `text.len()` returns byte length, not display-column width, so multi-byte Unicode identifiers (e.g., Chinese/Japanese characters, emoji) will have their byte length over-counted relative to the line width, causing them to fall through to `Never` when `IfFits` would be appropriate. Consider using a named constant for the threshold and using `text.chars().count()` or a proper Unicode column-width measurement for the comparison.

The `is_multiline_string` guard was intentionally included for implicit concatenations in the original code (with the comment `// Don't wrap triple quoted strings`). The new code removes this guard: implicit concatenated strings now always return `Multiline` regardless of whether they are multiline. This means triple-quoted implicit concatenated strings (e.g., `"""foo""" """bar"""`) will now be treated as `Multiline` and may get wrapped in parentheses, changing prior formatting behavior. If this is intentional it should be documented; if not, restore the `is_multiline_string` check for the implicit concatenation branch.

Same issue as in `expr_name.rs`: the `> 10` byte threshold is a magic number with no rationale, and `text.len()` is byte length not display-column width. For string literals this is particularly relevant since long string constants with multi-byte characters may be measured incorrectly, failing to parenthesize when they should.

`quick_test` appears to be a debugging helper left over from development rather than a proper regression test: the input and expected output are both the same for-loop snippet, and the test body constructs an assertion that mimics the test fixture infrastructure manually. This test will silently mislead future contributors and should either be turned into a proper fixture-based test (like the snapshot tests) or removed.

The bail-out condition only checks `jobIndex === -1`, but `workerIndex` can also be `-1` when all worker slots are busy and no free worker exists. In that case the loop finds an eligible job, fails both `findIndex` calls (returning -1), sets `jobIndex` to the job's index, and breaks. The subsequent `this._workerSlots[workerIndex].busy = true` (workerIndex === -1) will throw a TypeError. The old code had an explicit `if (index === -1) return` for this case. Fix: change the bail-out to `if (jobIndex === -1 || workerIndex === -1) return;`.

The validation block for global `config.workers` was removed from `validateConfig` (it checked `workers <= 0` and invalid string formats) but was not replaced anywhere for the global config. The new validation in `validateProject` only covers project-level workers. As a result, `workers: 0` or `workers: -5` in the root config now silently passes validation; `resolveWorkers` just returns the raw number, and Playwright will attempt to run with 0 or negative workers. Restore the `<= 0` check in `validateConfig` (or call the same validation helper for both config and project).

Using a truthiness check `projectConfig.workers ?` treats `workers: 0` as 'no limit' (returns `undefined`) rather than surfacing the invalid value. Although the `validateProject` call happens before this and should reject `<= 0`, the falsy guard is subtle and inconsistent with how the global workers field is handled. Prefer an explicit `!== undefined` check for clarity and safety.

`toCollectionString` calls `collection.toArray(new Object[collection.size()])`, which materializes the entire collection into a new array before any truncation occurs. For very large collections this still risks OOM, directly undermining the purpose of this fix. Prefer iterating the collection directly with an early exit after MAX_COLLECTION_LOG_SIZE elements instead of converting to an array first.

`Integer.valueOf(String)` returns a boxed `Integer` that is immediately auto-unboxed; use `Integer.parseInt` for a primitive `int` field. More importantly, a malformed system property value throws `NumberFormatException` during class initialization, turning it into a `NoClassDefFoundError`/`ExceptionInInitializerError` that prevents the class from loading anywhere it is used. Consider wrapping in a try-catch with a sensible default fallback.

`LogHelper.toString(data)` is evaluated eagerly before SLF4J checks whether the WARN level is enabled, so the string is always built even when logging is off. Because `CommandData.toString()` is already being updated in this PR to use `LogHelper` internally, passing `data` directly (without the explicit `LogHelper.toString` wrapper) would restore lazy evaluation while still being safe. The same applies to the equivalent call a few lines below and in `CommandAsyncService`.

The centering formula `(bounds.width / 2) - (box.width / 2)` can be off by one pixel compared to the correct `(bounds.width - box.width) / 2`. When `bounds.width` is even and `box.width` is odd (e.g. 1920 and 101), integer division gives 910 instead of 909. The same issue exists on the vertical axis at line 135. Prefer: `box.x = bounds.x + (bounds.width - box.width) / 2;`

When both opposing horizontal anchors are set with an explicit (non-zero) width, margins are now silently ignored. This differs from the single-anchor behaviour where margins shift the position. A client anchoring to both sides with a fixed width and non-zero margins reasonably expects the margins to offset the centred position. Consider applying symmetric or relevant margin offsets rather than dropping them entirely. The same issue applies to the vertical axis at line 160.

Wrong inner method called: `test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_external_after` delegates to `_test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_system_inner` instead of `_test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_external_inner`. This means the 'external AFTER' scenario is never tested, and the 'system AFTER' scenario is exercised twice (redundantly by `test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_system_after` as well).

Mutating the global `sys.modules` by deleting all non-system modules before each zip load is not thread-safe. In a concurrent Airflow environment (scheduler threads, webserver), another thread may be actively executing code from a module that is deleted here, leading to `ImportError` or `AttributeError` mid-flight. Consider using a per-zip isolated import mechanism (e.g., a custom `importlib.machinery.ModuleSpec` or a dedicated `importlib.util.LazyLoader`) rather than mutating the shared global state.

`mod_overrides` is only populated for root-level `.py`/`.pyc` files (the `if head: continue` guard skips all package subdirectories). So if a zip provides a package (e.g. `support_lib/__init__.py`), the package name `support_lib` is never added to `mod_overrides`. If a same-named package exists on the system path, `_is_system_module` will return True for it and it will NOT be dropped before the zip is loaded, so the zip's version silently loses. This is precisely the scenario described in the bug report (packages, not flat modules). Fix: also add the top-level directory name of any `.py`/`.pyc` path inside a subdirectory to `mod_overrides`.

`if not self.has_logged or True:` always evaluates to True (the `or True` short-circuits unconditionally). The anti-spam guard (`has_logged`) is completely bypassed, so every skipped non-DAG file produces a log line. The `or True` appears to be a leftover debug override. Remove it to restore the original intent.

`_is_system_module` returns `False` (treat as non-system / drop it) for any module whose `spec.origin` does not start with a known `_system_path` entry. Editable installs (`pip install -e .`) and virtualenv paths not present in `sys.path` at module-import time will fail to match, causing legitimate platform dependencies (e.g. a project's own library installed with `-e`) to be dropped from `sys.modules` on every zip load. The `return False` fallback should probably be `return True` (conservative: keep unknown modules) unless the intent is to be maximally aggressive.

If `image` is falsy but `err` is also null (an edge case the Cloudinary SDK shouldn't produce, but defensive code should handle), the Promise neither resolves nor rejects and will hang indefinitely. Add a `reject(new Error('No image returned from Cloudinary'))` in this branch to ensure the Promise always settles.

`upload_chunked_stream` uses a default chunk size of 20 MB. For environments with strict memory constraints or slow/unreliable networks, operators may need to tune this. Consider forwarding a configurable `chunk_size` option (e.g. from `customConfig` or a top-level provider option) so users can adjust it without forking the provider.

The job condition references `inputs.run-task-sdk-tests` but that input is never declared in the `workflow_call.inputs` block (only `runs-on-as-json-default`, `image-tag`, `canary-run`, `default-python-version`, and `python-versions` are declared). An undeclared workflow_call input evaluates to an empty string, so this condition is always false and the job is permanently skipped. Add `run-task-sdk-tests: { description: '...', required: true, type: string }` to the inputs section.

The condition `matrix.run-tests` references a matrix key that does not exist — the only matrix dimension is `python-version`. This expression will always be falsy, so the actual test execution step will never run even if the job is triggered. This appears to be a copy-paste artifact; the condition should be removed or replaced with a valid one.

The placeholder file is created at `task_sdk/src/airflow/__init__.py` (the shared namespace-package level) rather than at `task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/__init__.py` (the actual package level). This is inconsistent with the `providers` case, which places the placeholder at `providers/src/airflow/providers/__init__.py`, keeping `airflow/` as a pure namespace package. Adding `__init__.py` at the `airflow/` level in this source tree can prevent Python from merging the `airflow` namespace across multiple installed distributions, potentially causing import failures. The same pattern is duplicated in `Dockerfile` and `Dockerfile.ci`. The fix is to use `touch ./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/__init__.py` (and update the trap accordingly).

The `readme` field is commented out (`#readme = "README.md"`), so the package will be published to PyPI without a long description. Even though the README content is currently a placeholder, wiring it up now avoids a missing-metadata gap and ensures the field isn't forgotten once real content is added.

Stray `w` character at the end of the file makes this invalid TypeScript. The snapshot captures a spurious `ExpressionStatement` for `w`, so the test is not cleanly verifying the intended fix — it passes despite malformed input. Remove the `w` and regenerate the snapshot.
Missing newline at end of file. While not a correctness issue, many editors and linters flag this, and it is inconsistent with typical test fixture formatting.

The original bug report's reproducer uses `declare namespace`, but both new test cases only cover `export declare class`. Consider adding a test for the `declare namespace { export class { constructor({...}?: ...) } }` shape to confirm `in_declare` propagates correctly through nested ambient scopes.

`slidingSample` is not nulled after `Expire()`. If `HitObject.Samples` is empty, `firstSample` is null and the field retains a reference to the now-expired object. A subsequent `Tracking` change fires `updateSlidingSample`, which calls `slidingSample?.Play()` — the null-conditional won't protect here since the field is non-null, just expired. Fix: set `slidingSample = null` immediately after `slidingSample?.Expire();`.

`Ball.X` is in the slider's local coordinate space (relative to the slider's origin), not the absolute playfield X position. Dividing by `OsuPlayfield.BASE_SIZE.X` produces an incorrect stereo balance for any slider not anchored at the playfield origin. The absolute position of the ball on the playfield should be used instead (e.g. via a coordinate-space transform to the playfield).

`TypeInfoGenericCell` is imported here but is never defined in `utility.rs` (which only exports `TypeInfoCell`). This is an unresolved import that will cause a compilation error. The import should be removed or `TypeInfoGenericCell` needs to be defined/exported.

Using `TypeInfoCell::non_generic()` for a generic tuple impl is incorrect. In Rust, `static` items inside generic functions/methods are shared across all monomorphizations (not duplicated per type-parameter combination). This means `(i32, f32)::type_info()` and `(String, u64)::type_info()` share the same CELL and will both return whichever TypeInfo was initialized first—corrupting type info for all but the first-initialized arity variant. The `impl_typed` helper in `impls.rs` explicitly branches on `is_generic` to select `generic()` vs `non_generic()`. The macro here should use `TypeInfoCell::generic()` for all arities with at least one type parameter.

The return type should be `-> &'static TypeInfo`. Both paths always return a `'static` reference: the `NonGeneric` path via `OnceBox` in a static, and the `Generic` path via `Box::leak`. The current signature `-> &TypeInfo` with an elided lifetime implies the reference only lives as long as `&self`, which is misleading and forces callers to rely on the compiler inferring `'static` from the static cell context rather than from the signature. Changing the return to `&'static TypeInfo` makes the contract explicit.

The fix is correct, but correctness depends implicitly on the coordinator's debouncer being configured with `immediate=True`. `async_request_refresh()` is the debounced/indirect path; using `coordinator.async_refresh()` directly would be more explicit and decouple the config-flow check from the coordinator's internal debouncer configuration. If the debouncer is ever changed to `immediate=False`, this call would return before the fetch completes and both `coordinator.data` and `coordinator.last_exception` would remain `None`, silently causing every config-flow attempt to abort with `no_status`.

The error check only handles `UpdateFailed` and `asyncio.TimeoutError`. Any other exception that propagates from `_async_update_data` (e.g., an unexpected library error not covered by the `OSError`/`IncompleteReadError` guard in the coordinator) will be caught by the coordinator's broad `except Exception` handler, stored in `last_exception`, leave `coordinator.data` as `None`, and fall through to the `no_status` abort on line 64 — giving the user a misleading 'no status found' message for what is really a connection failure. Using `not coordinator.last_update_success` as the primary condition (covering all failure types) would be more robust.

The snapshot contains a hardcoded macOS-specific temp path (`/var/folders/nt/.../T/.tmp3nlq1y`). This test will fail on Linux CI and any non-macOS machine. The path should be normalized using whatever placeholder mechanism the other tests use (e.g., `[TEMP_DIR]`).

The test is named `requires_python_direct_url` and its doc comment says "Raise an error when...", but the TODO acknowledges it currently succeeds (i.e., the enforcement is not implemented for `pip sync`). This PR fixes `pip_compile` and `pip_install` but leaves `pip sync` broken, and the test encodes the broken behavior as the expected baseline. Consider either fixing `pip sync` or renaming the test to make the gap explicit (e.g., `requires_python_direct_url_not_enforced`).

The previous code had a fast path for wheel direct URLs that extracted the version directly from the wheel filename without hitting the index. That branch is now removed, and all non-editable direct URLs go through `self.index.distributions.wait(...)`. If wheel URL metadata was never populated into the index for this code path (it wasn't previously needed there), the `wait` call will block indefinitely. Verify that the index-population code path is triggered for wheel direct URLs before this function is reached.

`dhcp4_mtu_reset_loop_detected` is set to true but never cleared for the lifetime of the Link object. A spurious carrier drop (unrelated to the MTU change) that happens within the 10-second timer window will permanently disable UseMTU= for this interface, even across DHCP lease cycles. Consider resetting the flag when the interface goes through a full down/up cycle or when a configurable retry threshold is exceeded rather than on the first detected event.

`link_set_mtu` now returns 1 (instead of 0) on success when a netlink request is actually sent, changing its contract from the typical systemd convention of 0=success. The two existing callers in `link_configure_mtu` propagate this value via `return link_set_mtu(...)`, and their caller checks `if (r < 0)`, so they are accidentally safe. But the tristate semantics (negative=error, 0=no-op, 1=request sent) are not documented and could easily mislead future callers into treating 1 as an error.

If `sd_event_source_set_destroy_callback` fails and the function returns early, `link->dhcp4_mtu_reset_loop_detector` is left pointing to a live event source that has no destroy callback and no corresponding `link_ref`. On the next call to `dhcp_set_mtu`, the `else` branch will reuse this source (resetting and re-enabling its timer), but without the destroy callback the link lifecycle accounting is inconsistent. The event source should be explicitly unref'd and the field set to NULL in this error path.

Division by zero if `size == 0`: `SIZE_MAX / size` is undefined behavior. Standard `calloc` permits `size == 0`, so callers may legitimately pass zero. Add a guard: `if (size == 0 || SIZE_MAX / size < nmemb) return NULL;`

`num * size` can silently overflow `size_t` before being passed to `ztrycalloc_usable`. Although the `hdr_calloc` wrapper checks for overflow before calling this function via the function pointer, `zcalloc_num` is declared as a public API (exported in zmalloc.h) and any direct caller bypasses that guard. The multiplication should be checked internally, e.g., check `num != 0 && SIZE_MAX / num < size` and call `zmalloc_oom_handler` or return NULL on overflow.

The `extra_authorize_data` property added to `MockOAuth2Implementation` is dead code: this class overrides `async_generate_authorize_url` with a hard-coded URL and never calls `super()`, so the property is never consulted. As a result, the new `update_query(self.extra_authorize_data)` path inside `LocalOAuth2Implementation.async_generate_authorize_url` (config_entry_oauth2_flow.py:140) is not actually tested at the implementation level. A test using a `LocalOAuth2Implementation` subclass that overrides `extra_authorize_data` and asserts the returned URL contains those keys would provide real coverage.

`update_query` merges extra data on top of the already-built query string, meaning a subclass `extra_authorize_data` can silently override `state` (the CSRF token), `client_id`, or `redirect_uri`. Note that `AbstractOAuth2FlowHandler.async_step_auth` (line 251) applies a *second* `update_query` from the flow-handler level on the same URL, so the `state` value set in this method can be clobbered twice — once here and once there. At minimum, document that reserved OAuth2 keys (`state`, `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, `response_type`) must not appear in `extra_authorize_data`.

`PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` is the name mandated by the POSIX standard (IEEE 1003.1-2017). Renaming it to `POSIX_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` silently breaks any existing application code that uses the standard macro name. The deprecated Kconfig wrapper in `Kconfig.deprecated` handles the Kconfig namespace migration, but this C macro name should remain `PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER`. At minimum, keep the old name as an alias.
Copy-paste error: the description says "pthread_spinlock_t support" but this is the deprecated rwlock option. Should read "pthread_rwlock_t support" (or similar).
The comment references `POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS_INITIALIZER`, which does not exist. The actual new macro name introduced by this PR is `POSIX_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER`.

The second close-all loop (lines 197-198) closes fds 0, 1, and 2 before calling `exit(0)`. The C `exit()` function flushes and closes the stdio streams (stdout, stderr), which requires their underlying file descriptors to still be open. Calling `exit()` after these fds are already closed is undefined behavior per POSIX. Use `_exit(0)` instead, which skips C library cleanup and is the standard choice for the child side of a daemonizing fork. Alternatively, remove the redundant second close-all loop entirely — the OS reclaims all file descriptors when the process exits.

Changing from `open(error.log)` to `open("/dev/null")` redirects the child's stderr to /dev/null. The `error()` macro is implemented via `error_int()` in log.c (lines 779-790), which writes exclusively to `fprintf(stderr, ...)`. As a result, any error output from the child — including the 'Cannot become session id leader' message — will now be silently discarded rather than captured in error.log. The PR description says errors are moved *to* error.log, but the code does the opposite. Revert the open target to `error.log` (or the configured log path) to preserve error visibility.

When escaping remotely, `reviveQueueOffset` is hardcoded to 0 for all CKs. If this offset is used downstream to look up or deduplicate CK entries (e.g., to match an ACK to its CK), every remotely stored CK will have the same effective offset, which could break matching logic. Consider using a sentinel value that callers explicitly handle, or documenting precisely which code paths skip offset-based lookup for remote CKs.

The new catch block swallows all exceptions and returns normally. If an exception fires before `getMessageTmpResult` is assigned, the method returns `restNum` as if the queue was successfully (but empty) polled. This silently drops the failure: the queue lock offset may be advanced or not updated consistently, and callers receive a misleadingly successful response. At minimum, consider propagating the exception or returning an error code so the caller can retry or back off.

All five test methods share the same static `CONSUME_GROUP`. Consumer group offsets are persisted on the broker across test cases within the same JVM run. If tests execute sequentially and share broker state, a later test may resume from the offset left by an earlier test, causing it to miss expected messages and produce incorrect assertions. Each test should use a distinct consumer group (e.g., append the topic name or a counter).

`Thread.sleep(10000L)` is used as the sole guard before asserting that no retry messages arrived. If the ack/revive cycle takes longer than 10 s (e.g., under load), the assertion passes prematurely (false positive). Replace with an `await()` condition that either polls for the absence of retry messages with a bounded timeout, or explicitly waits until the revive cycle has completed.

`msg.setReconsumeTimes(0)` inside the message listener has no effect: the broker tracks reconsume counts server-side and ignores this field on the received `MessageExt`. This looks like a debug leftover that could mislead future readers into thinking it controls retry behavior. It should be removed.

Function name contains Cyrillic characters ('шт_кгду'), which appears to be an accidental paste (likely a transliteration of 'in_rule'). Non-ASCII function names can fail in some shell environments and make the test unrunnable or undiscoverable. Rename to something like `test_tags_propagated_to_run_with_exec_info_in_rule`.

The guard only checks `!= Runtime.NONE`, but four out of five calls in the new tests pass `null` as the 'no requirements' sentinel. `null != Runtime.NONE` is `true`, so `castSkylarkDictOrNoneToDict(null, ...)` would be invoked, risking NPE or silent incorrect behavior. The condition should be `executionRequirementsUnchecked != null && executionRequirementsUnchecked != Runtime.NONE`, or the contract should be documented and enforced (e.g., Preconditions.checkNotNull) so callers are required to pass Runtime.NONE instead of null.

`getFilteredExecutionInfo` introduces Skylark-specific types (`SkylarkDict`, `Runtime`, `EvalException`) into `TargetUtils`, a general `packages`-layer utility. This couples a build-model utility to the Starlark evaluator and forces a checked Starlark exception to cross the package boundary. The Skylark-specific dict-casting and NONE-sentinel logic would be better kept in `SkylarkActionFactory` (or a new Skylark helper), with `TargetUtils` exposing a clean `getFilteredExecutionInfo(Map<String,String> explicitRequirements, Rule rule)` taking an already-resolved map.

All three test functions use `>>` to append to the same `test/BUILD` file and all define a target named `"test"`. If the test framework does not create a fresh workspace before each function (there is no explicit `setup_clean_workspace` call in these functions), later functions will see a BUILD file with duplicate target names and fail with a confusing error. Each function should use `>` (overwrite) instead of `>>` (append), or each should use its own subdirectory.

Potential stale entries in `notebook_cells`: when a notebook is opened, all its cells are registered in `notebook_cells` (line 330-333). With this change, closing a notebook no longer removes those entries — they only get cleaned up if `textDocument/didClose` is sent for every cell. If the client omits any cell-level close notification (as interactive sessions appear to do inconsistently), those cell URLs remain in `notebook_cells` mapping to a notebook that no longer exists in `self.documents`. A subsequent lookup via `key_from_url` would then classify stale URLs as `NotebookCell`, and any attempt to access their parent notebook would fail. Mitigating this by draining `notebook_cells` of entries whose notebook URL equals the just-removed URL would make cleanup unconditional without reintroducing the original error.

The `/*#__PURE__*/` annotation is added to `Y1`'s `mixin2(Y)` extends call, but `C1` on line 11 has the same pattern (`mixin2(Y, ...)`) without the annotation on the outer call. This inconsistency means `C1` is still treated as having side effects and won't be tree-shaken, while `Y1` will be. If the intent is to test both annotated and unannotated scenarios, that should be documented; otherwise `C1`'s outer `mixin2` call should also be annotated for consistency.

The PR fixes a scope bug where a class expression name that shadows an import is incorrectly replaced with the import reference inside the class body (e.g. `C.name` becomes `importedModule.a.name`). This test change only adds a `/*#__PURE__*/` annotation and does not exercise that specific scenario. A test case matching the bug report pattern — a named class expression whose name shadows an import, with access to the class name via a static method/property — appears to be missing, which leaves the core fix without direct regression coverage.

Wrong parameter asserted: the function never uses `data`; it uses `userdata` (via `network = userdata`). The assertion should be `assert(userdata)`, matching the pattern in `config_parse_can_control_mode`. The current `assert(data)` is only accidentally safe because the gperf offset is 0 (making `data == userdata`); it provides no guard on the pointer actually dereferenced and will mislead future maintainers.

Removing ('core', 'sql_alchemy_conn') from sensitive_config_values breaks the deprecated fallback for _cmd and _secret variants. When the deprecated lookup path calls _get_env_var_option('core', 'sql_alchemy_conn'), _get_cmd_option('core', 'sql_alchemy_conn'), and _get_secret_option('core', 'sql_alchemy_conn'), all three check `(section, key) in self.sensitive_config_values` with section='core' — which is now False. This silently ignores AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN_CMD, AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN_SECRET, and [core] sql_alchemy_conn_cmd/sql_alchemy_conn_secret in airflow.cfg for anyone who hasn't migrated to [database] yet. Fix: also keep ('core', 'sql_alchemy_conn') in sensitive_config_values during the deprecation period.

Stray '2' at the end of the YAML value. The default will be parsed as the string '"utf-8"2' rather than '"utf-8"', which will break encoding configuration for the database section. Should be: `default: "utf-8"`

The model ID string 'roborock.vacuum.s4' is unverified. Other constants like ROCKROBO_S5_MAX='roborock.vacuum.s5e' (non-obvious 's5e' suffix) show that model IDs can be non-intuitive. This should be confirmed against the actual string the device reports (e.g., via the miio library source or a user-provided device diagnostic). If wrong, the fix has no effect and the KeyError will persist.

No test covers the new model routing. The bug is that S4 was missing from MODELS_VACUUM, causing async_create_miio_device_and_coordinator to return early (KEY_DEVICE never set) while get_platforms still routed to VACUUM_PLATFORMS. A test asserting that a config entry with model='roborock.vacuum.s4' results in a successful vacuum platform setup would prevent this class of regression. The PR checklist also explicitly marks tests as not added.

Malformed HTML: the `href` attribute is missing its closing quote. `use_extension>` should be `use_extension">`. As written, the attribute value and everything up to the next `"` will be treated as part of the URL, breaking the rendered link.
Missing space in string concatenation: `"...names of all"` concatenated with `"repositories generated..."` produces `"...names of allrepositories..."`. Add a trailing space: `"...names of all "`.
Same missing-space issue as in `root_module_direct_deps`: `"...names of all"` + `"repositories generated..."` renders as `"...names of allrepositories..."`. Add a trailing space.
